corrections.claim: family_notes: 'Nemotron 3 configs set mamba_ssm_cache_dtype=float32'
corrections.was: Stated as applying to all Nemotron 3 configs
corrections.should_be: True for Nano-30B-A3B, Super-120B, Ultra-550B configs (verified mamba_ssm_cache_dtype='float32'), but the NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-4B-BF16 config.json contains NO mamba_ssm_cache_dtype field at all (older-style config, transformers_version 4.53.0). Cosmetic; does not change any variant row.

corrections.claim: family_notes: 'the NVFP4 repos quantize experts to NVFP4 + Mamba projections to FP8'
corrections.was: implies all experts are NVFP4
corrections.should_be: Only ROUTED experts are NVFP4 (group_size 16); SHARED experts (shared_experts.up_proj/down_proj) are FP8, along with Mamba in_proj/out_proj. The NVFP4 repos also set kv_cache_quant_algo=FP8 (per hf_quant_config.json), which a VRAM calculator should reflect when modeling the NVFP4 deployment artifact. Marginal effect on bytes-per-weight estimates.

corrections.claim: family_notes: excluded model named 'Nemotron-3-Nano-Omni-30B-A3B'
corrections.was: Nemotron-3-Nano-Omni-30B-A3B
corrections.should_be: Actual HF repo ids are nvidia/Nemotron-3-Nano-Omni-30B-A3B-Reasoning-{BF16,FP8,NVFP4} (any-to-any). Trivial naming nuance; correctly excluded per scope.

missing: Nemotron-Mini-4B-Instruct (nvidia/Nemotron-Mini-4B-Instruct, ~432K monthly downloads — higher than several included variants): dense ~4.2B Minitron-pruned Nemotron-4, 4K context, llama-style arch; still very popular for local/edge use and arguably in scope for a VRAM calculator.

Llama-3_1-Nemotron-51B-Instruct (DeciLM/Puzzle-NAS): the one Llama-Nemotron NAS model where replace_with_linear is actually used — verified from config block_configs: 80 blocks, 8 attention no_op, 18 attention replaced-with-LINEAR, so only 54 KV-cached attention blocks (and 18 linear blocks hold no KV). If the calculator covers prev-gen NAS models, this needs its own KV rule; the researcher's 'replace_with_linear=false' claim was correctly scoped to 49B/253B only, but the 51B exception went unmentioned.
NVIDIA-Nemotron-Nano-9B-v2-Japanese (~295K monthly downloads): same nemotron_h architecture/dims as Nano-9B-v2; trivial to support as an alias row.
Nemotron-Cascade-2-30B-A3B (~46K downloads): verified NemotronHForCausalLM, 52 layers, 2 KV heads, max_pos 262144 — a derivative sharing Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B geometry (alias row).
NVIDIA-Nemotron-Labs-3-Elastic-30B-A3B-{BF16,FP8,NVFP4}: Nemotron Elastic many-in-one variant of the 30B-A3B (nested submodels extractable at smaller sizes).
Base repos exist with identical geometry: NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-{Nano-30B-A3B,Super-120B-A12B,Ultra-550B-A55B}-Base-BF16; also NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-4B-GGUF for llama.cpp users.

Mamba2 SSM-state VRAM was described qualitatively but never quantified — for a hybrid-model VRAM calculator this is the dominant 'cache' term per sequence at fp32 (mamba_ssm_cache_dtype=float32): per mamba layer ssm_state = mamba_num_heads * mamba_head_dim * ssm_state_size * 4B. Computed from verified configs: Ultra-550B ~402 MB/seq (48 layers x 256x64x128 fp32), Super-120B ~168 MB/seq, Nano-30B ~48 MB/seq, plus small conv states ((d_inner + 2*n_groups*ssm_state)*conv_kernel per layer). This dwarfs the ~12 KB/token attention KV at short-to-mid contexts and should be in the calculator.
FP8 KV-cache quantization is the shipped default in the FP8/NVFP4 deployment repos (kv_cache_quant_algo=FP8 in hf_quant_config.json) — relevant bytes-per-KV option.

Older dense Llama-arch Nemotrons still in use (standard Llama VRAM math, may be deliberately out of scope): Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1 (~17K dl/mo), Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70B-Instruct-HF (~13K dl/mo), Llama-3.1-Nemotron-8B-UltraLong-1M/2M/4M.
verification.summary: Re-verified all 8 variants directly against primary sources: fetched every config.json from HuggingFace raw, pulled exact parameter counts from the HF API safetensors metadata, programmatically counted hybrid_override_pattern / layers_block_type / DeciLM block_configs, and checked model cards for context-length, active-params, release-date, and NVFP4 claims. Result: essentially 100% of numeric claims are correct. Confirmed exactly: Nano-4B 3.974B/42L (4 attn+21 Mamba+17 MLP, 40H/8KV/128hd, hidden 3136, 262144 ctx); Nano-30B 31.578B/52L (6 attn+23 Mamba+23 MoE, 32H/2KV/128hd, 128+1 experts top-6, card states 3.5B active, 1M ctx with 256K HF default, released 2025-12-15); Super-120B 123.611B/88L (8 attn+40 Mamba+40 MoE, 512+1 experts top-22, moe_latent_size 1024, mtp '*E', NVFP4-pretrained, released 2026-03-11); Ultra-550B 560.525B/108L (layers_block_type counts exactly 12 attention+48 mamba+48 moe, 64H/2KV/128hd, hidden 8192, latent 2048, mtp ['attention','moe'], 55B active, released 2026-06-04); Nano-9B-v2 8.888B/56L (4 attn+27 M+25 MLP); Nano-12B-v2 12.310B/62L (6 attn+28 M+28 MLP); Llama-49B-v1.5 49.867B, 49 of 80 blocks with real attention (31 no_op, 0 linear, n_heads_in_group=8 → 8 KV heads); Llama-253B 253.401B, 64 of 162 with attention (98 no_op, 0 linear, n_heads_in_group=16 → 8 KV heads). sliding_window is null in every config; all hybrids are model_type nemotron_h/NemotronHForCausalLM; the 12 KB/token Ultra KV arithmetic checks out; 49B-v1.5 downloads ~787K matches. The Ultra-550B KV-layer count (12 of 108) — the highest-risk number — was confirmed by counting the literal layers_block_type array. Only cosmetic notes-level corrections found (Nano-4B config lacks mamba_ssm_cache_dtype; in NVFP4 repos shared experts are FP8 not NVFP4 and KV cache ships FP8-quantized; Omni repo naming). Biggest gaps to flag for the calculator: the unquantified Mamba2 SSM-state-per-sequence term (~402 MB/seq fp32 on Ultra, ~168 MB Super, ~48 MB Nano-30B) and the Llama-3.1-Nemotron-51B NAS model whose 18 linear-attention blocks break the 49B/253B KV rule, plus high-download misses Nemotron-Mini-4B-Instruct and Nano-9B-v2-Japanese.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 2080 Ti
devices.usable_note: Discrete VRAM, no ECC on GeForce. Effectively full 11 GB allocatable; subtract ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context overhead per process, plus ~0.5-1.5 GB if the card drives a Windows desktop (DWM/WDDM reservation; DXGI app budget is typically ~90% of physical VRAM). Headless Linux: nearly all 11 GB usable by llama.cpp.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 3080
devices.usable_note: Two factory configs: 10 GB @ 320-bit/19 Gbps = 760 GB/s, and 12 GB @ 384-bit/19 Gbps = 912 GB/s (launched Jan 2022). Discrete VRAM, no ECC; subtract ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, plus ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop/WDDM reservation if driving displays; headless Linux gets nearly full capacity.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 3090
devices.usable_note: 24 GB @ 384-bit/19.5 Gbps = 936 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC. Subtract ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context per process; Windows desktop reserves ~0.5-1.5 GB on the display GPU (DXGI budget ~90% of VRAM); headless Linux ~23.5 GB usable.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 4080
devices.usable_note: 16 GB @ 256-bit/22.4 Gbps = 716.8 GB/s. The announced 'RTX 4080 12GB' was unlaunched and rebranded RTX 4070 Ti, so 16 GB is the only RTX 4080 config (RTX 4080 SUPER also 16 GB @ 736 GB/s). Discrete VRAM, no ECC; ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation if driving displays.
devices.status: shipped (discontinued, Ada 2022; replaced by 4080 SUPER in 2024)

devices.name: GeForce RTX 4090
devices.usable_note: 24 GB @ 384-bit/21 Gbps = 1008 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC on GeForce. Subtract ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context per process; Windows DXGI budget ~90% of VRAM on the display GPU; headless Linux ~23.5 GB usable for LLM weights+KV.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5060
devices.usable_note: 8 GB @ 128-bit/28 Gbps = 448 GB/s. On an 8 GB card the fixed overheads matter proportionally more: ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context + ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation can leave only ~6-7 GB for model+KV on a display GPU; headless Linux ~7.5 GB. Rumored 9GB variant (3x3GB GDDR7) NOT shipped and disputed by leakers.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5060 Ti
devices.usable_note: Two factory configs: 8 GB and 16 GB, both 128-bit/28 Gbps = 448 GB/s (identical bandwidth). Discrete VRAM, no ECC; ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation if driving displays; headless Linux nearly full capacity.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5070
devices.usable_note: 12 GB @ 192-bit/28 Gbps = 672 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC; ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation if driving displays.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5070 Ti
devices.usable_note: 16 GB @ 256-bit/28 Gbps = 896 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC; ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation if driving displays.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5080
devices.usable_note: 16 GB @ 256-bit/30 Gbps = 960 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC; ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context, ~0.5-1.5 GB Windows desktop reservation if driving displays.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5090
devices.usable_note: 32 GB @ 512-bit/28 Gbps = 1792 GB/s. Discrete VRAM, no ECC on GeForce. Subtract ~0.3-0.6 GB CUDA context per process; Windows DXGI budget ~90% of VRAM on the display GPU; headless Linux ~31.5 GB usable.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5070 SUPER (rumored, NOT shipped)
devices.usable_note: Does NOT exist as a product as of June 2026. Leaked spec: 18 GB via 6x3GB GDDR7 @ 192-bit/28 Gbps = 672 GB/s. Refresh delayed indefinitely per NVIDIA partner communications (GDDR7 shortage); latest rumors say Q3 2026 or CES 2027.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5070 Ti SUPER (rumored, NOT shipped)
devices.usable_note: Does NOT exist as a product as of June 2026. Leaked spec: 24 GB via 8x3GB GDDR7 @ 256-bit/28 Gbps = 896 GB/s. Refresh delayed indefinitely (GDDR7 shortage); rumored Q3 2026 or CES 2027.

devices.name: GeForce RTX 5080 SUPER (rumored, NOT shipped)
devices.usable_note: Does NOT exist as a product as of June 2026. Leaked spec: 24 GB via 8x3GB GDDR7 @ 256-bit/32 Gbps = 1024 GB/s. Refresh delayed indefinitely (GDDR7 shortage, AI demand); rumored Q3 2026 or CES 2027.

research.vendor_notes: All GeForce cards listed use discrete (non-unified) VRAM, so raw capacity is essentially the usable capacity — there is no unified-memory cap analogous to macOS recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize. GeForce has no ECC option (no ECC carve-out, unlike workstation RTX/A-series where enabling ECC on GDDR reserves ~6-7% of VRAM). Practical deductions for LLM use: (1) CUDA context ~300-600 MB per process; (2) if the card drives a display on Windows, WDDM/DWM reserves memory and the DXGI 'budget' exposed to apps is typically ~90% of physical VRAM (~0.5-1.5 GB off the top); (3) headless Linux leaves ~200-500 MB to the driver, so llama.cpp users routinely allocate within ~0.5 GB of full capacity. Variant clarifications verified against vendor pages: RTX 3080 shipped in 10 GB (760 GB/s) and 12 GB (912 GB/s) factory configs; RTX 4080 is 16 GB only (the 'RTX 4080 12GB' was unlaunched and became RTX 4070 Ti — nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/news/12gb-4080-unlaunch/); RTX 5060 Ti ships in 8 GB and 16 GB at identical 448 GB/s. CRITICAL for the calculator: the RTX 50 SUPER refresh (5080 Super 24GB, 5070 Ti Super 24GB, 5070 Super 18GB, all 3GB-module GDDR7) had NOT shipped as of June 2026 — NVIDIA told partners it is delayed indefinitely due to the GDDR7/DRAM shortage from AI demand, with rumors pointing to Q3 2026 or CES 2027 (eteknix.com, tweaktown.com/news/109694, videocardz.com/newz/nvidia-geforce-rtx-50-super-series-now-rumored-for-ces-2027). Rumored 9GB 5060/5060 Ti variants also unshipped and disputed. Capacity/type/bus-width verified on NVIDIA product pages (nvidia.com 50-series pages); bandwidths from NVIDIA's RTX Blackwell architecture whitepaper (images.nvidia.com/aem-dam/Solutions/geforce/blackwell/nvidia-rtx-blackwell-gpu-architecture.pdf) and TechPowerUp/TechSpot databases.

missing: GeForce RTX 5050 (desktop): SHIPPING since late July 2025, the only desktop Blackwell card with GDDR6 — 8 GB GDDR6 @ 128-bit/20 Gbps = 320 GB/s, $249. Researcher's 50-series list omits it entirely. Sources: https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/news/rtx-5050-desktop-gpu-and-laptops/ , https://www.tomshardware.com/pc-components/gpus/nvidia-rtx-5050-puts-blackwell-within-reach-of-more-gamers-at-usd249-entry-level-50-series-launches-in-late-july

GeForce RTX 3060 12GB: arguably the most popular budget local-LLM card (12 GB GDDR6 @ 192-bit = 360 GB/s); also exists as an 8 GB variant (128-bit, 240 GB/s) that traps users — important for a VRAM calculator.
GeForce RTX 4060 Ti 16GB: very popular budget LLM pick (16 GB GDDR6 @ 128-bit = 288 GB/s); also 8 GB variant. Two memory_options like the 5060 Ti.
Entire RTX 40 mid-range missing: RTX 4070 / 4070 SUPER (12 GB, 504 GB/s), RTX 4070 Ti (12 GB, 504 GB/s), RTX 4070 Ti SUPER (16 GB @ 256-bit = 672 GB/s). The 4070 Ti SUPER 16GB is a common LLM card.

GeForce RTX 4080 SUPER as its own entry (16 GB GDDR6X @ 23 Gbps = 736 GB/s) — currently only mentioned inside the RTX 4080 note; it is a distinct shipped SKU. Verified 736 GB/s: https://www.thefpsreview.com/gpu-family/nvidia-geforce-rtx-4080-super-gpu-family-specifications/
GeForce RTX 3080 Ti (12 GB GDDR6X, 912 GB/s) and RTX 3090 Ti (24 GB GDDR6X @ 21 Gbps = 1008 GB/s) — the 3090 Ti is a 24 GB card LLM users actually buy used.
Titan RTX (24 GB GDDR6, 672 GB/s, Turing) — older but still a 24 GB consumer-purchasable card found in used builds.

Laptop GPUs absent (e.g. RTX 5090 Laptop 24 GB GDDR7, RTX 4090 Laptop 16 GB) — if the calculator targets 'can I run it' for consumers, laptop SKUs with same marketing names but different VRAM are a major confusion source worth an explicit table or disclaimer.

Technique/nuance worth adding (supports, not contradicts, researcher): per NVIDIA RTX Blackwell whitepaper v1.1, GDDR7 has on-die ECC always enabled on GeForce RTX 50-series with no performance hit, no toggle, and no VRAM carve-out — so the 'no ECC carve-out on GeForce' usable-capacity assumption remains correct even on Blackwell.
verification.summary: Adversarially re-verified all 14 device entries against primary sources; found zero numeric errors. Bandwidth/capacity/bus-width claims confirmed directly from NVIDIA's RTX Blackwell architecture whitepaper v1.1 spec tables (downloaded and text-extracted): RTX 5090 32GB/512-bit/28Gbps/1792 GB/s, RTX 5080 16GB/256-bit/30Gbps/960 GB/s, RTX 5070 Ti 16GB/256-bit/28Gbps/896 GB/s, RTX 5070 12GB/192-bit/28Gbps/672 GB/s, plus the comparison columns confirming RTX 3090 24GB/19.5Gbps/936 GB/s, RTX 4090 24GB/21Gbps/1008 GB/s, RTX 3080 10GB/19Gbps/760 GB/s, RTX 4080 16GB/22.4Gbps/716.8 GB/s. NVIDIA product pages confirm 5090 32GB/512-bit and 5080 16GB/256-bit. RTX 5060 8GB and 5060 Ti 8/16GB at 128-bit/28Gbps/448 GB/s confirmed via Tom's Hardware review, ASUS spec pages, TechPowerUp review. RTX 2080 Ti 11GB/352-bit/616 GB/s confirmed (EVGA/GamersNexus official launch specs). RTX 3080 12GB @ 384-bit/912 GB/s, launched Jan 11 2022, confirmed (TechSpot/Tom's Hardware). RTX 4080 12GB unlaunch->4070 Ti confirmed (NVIDIA's own post). 4080 SUPER 736 GB/s note confirmed. The market-status claims hold as of June 2026: RTX 50 SUPER refresh (5080S 24GB@32Gbps, 5070TiS 24GB@28Gbps, 5070S 18GB@28Gbps) is NOT shipped — CES 2026 ended with no discrete GPU announcement, NVIDIA told partners the refresh is indefinitely delayed amid the GDDR7/DRAM shortage, with conflicting Q3-2026/CES-2027 rumors (TechPowerUp, TweakTown, KitGuru, VideoCardz); the leaked SUPER memory specs the researcher lists match kopite7kimi/MLID leaks exactly. Bonus confirmation: Blackwell whitepaper v1.1 states GDDR7 on-die ECC is always enabled with no VRAM carve-out, supporting the researcher's usable-capacity model. Main weakness is coverage, not accuracy: the shipping RTX 5050 (8GB GDDR6, 320 GB/s) is missing from the 50-series list, and the most popular budget LLM cards (RTX 3060 12GB, RTX 4060 Ti 16GB) plus the whole RTX 40 mid-range, 3080 Ti/3090 Ti, and laptop SKUs are absent. Key sources: https://images.nvidia.com/aem-dam/Solutions/geforce/blackwell/nvidia-rtx-blackwell-gpu-architecture.pdf ; https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/graphics-cards/50-series/rtx-5090/ ; https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/graphics-cards/50-series/rtx-5080/ ; https://www.tomshardware.com/pc-components/gpus/nvidia-geforce-rtx-5060-ti-16gb-review ; https://www.asus.com/us/motherboards-components/graphics-cards/prime/prime-rtx5060ti-16g/techspec/ ; https://www.techspot.com/review/2992-nvidia-geforce-rtx-5060/ ; https://www.tomshardware.com/news/nvidia-geforce-rtx-3080-12gb-launch ; https://www.evga.com/products/specs/gpu.aspx?pn=ead34e50-d15c-41e0-9a3e-014dc2d93bd0 ; https://www.techpowerup.com/342705/gddr7-shortage-could-stop-nvidia-geforce-rtx-50-series-super-rollout ; https://www.tweaktown.com/news/107546/heres-the-full-leaked-details-on-rtx-5080-super-rtx-5070-ti-super-rtx-5070-super-in-october/index.html ; https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/news/rtx-5050-desktop-gpu-and-laptops/

devices.name: RTX A6000 (Ampere)
devices.usable_note: Full 48GB exposed with ECC off (default). GDDR boards use inline ECC: enabling ECC via nvidia-smi reserves ~6.25% of VRAM for ECC bits and reduces bandwidth slightly (per NVIDIA vGPU/ECC docs).

devices.name: RTX 6000 Ada Generation
devices.usable_note: Full 48GB exposed with ECC off (default); inline-ECC reservation (~6.25%) applies only if ECC is enabled.

devices.name: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (Workstation / Max-Q / Server editions)
devices.usable_note: 96GB GDDR7 with ECC, 1792 GB/s on all three editions (Workstation 600W, Max-Q 300W, Server). GDDR7 inline ECC: enabling ECC reserves a slice of VRAM, same mechanism as other GDDR boards. Supports MIG (up to 4x 24GB instances).

devices.name: L40S
devices.usable_note: Datacenter GDDR board; ECC on by default in most deployments — inline ECC reserves a small fraction of the 48GB when enabled (nvidia-smi typically reports ~45-46GiB).

devices.name: A100 40GB (PCIe / SXM4)
devices.usable_note: HBM ECC is sideband — no capacity penalty; nvidia-smi exposes ~40,536 MiB.

devices.name: A100 80GB (PCIe / SXM4)
devices.usable_note: SXM4: 2039 GB/s; PCIe: 1935 GB/s. HBM ECC sideband, no capacity penalty.

devices.name: H100 SXM
devices.usable_note: nvidia-smi exposes ~81,559 MiB; HBM ECC sideband, no capacity loss.

devices.name: H100 NVL
devices.usable_note: 94GB per card (six active HBM3 stacks, partially provisioned vs H200's 141GB). NVIDIA also markets the NVLink-bridged pair as '188GB' — that is 2 cards.

devices.name: H200 (SXM / NVL)
devices.usable_note: SXM and NVL (PCIe, air-cooled) variants have identical 141GB / 4.8 TB/s memory. 141GB is itself a provisioned figure (six 24GB HBM3e stacks = 144GB raw).

devices.name: B200 (SXM, HGX/DGX B200)
devices.usable_note: 180GB is the shipping, GPU-exposed capacity (DGX B200 = 1,440GB total / 8 GPUs; Lenovo HGX B200 guide: 180GB, 7.7 TB/s). The 192GB figure from the GTC 2024 announcement is the raw 8x24GB stack capacity; production parts are provisioned to 180GB. NVIDIA marketing rounds bandwidth to 'up to 8 TB/s'.

devices.name: B300 (Blackwell Ultra, SXM, HGX/DGX B300)
devices.usable_note: 288GB is the marketed raw capacity (8x36GB 12-Hi HBM3e). NVIDIA's own DGX B300 page lists 2.1TB total for 8 GPUs (~262-270GB/GPU) and third-party comparisons cite 270GB exposed — same ~6.25% provisioning pattern as B200 (192 to 180). Treat ~270GB as GPU-exposed until NVIDIA publishes a per-GPU datasheet figure.

devices.name: GB200 Grace Blackwell Superchip
devices.memory_type: HBM3e (GPU) + up to 480GB LPDDR5X (Grace CPU, coherent)
devices.usable_note: memory_gb is aggregate HBM3e across 2 Blackwell GPUs (186GB each, ~8 TB/s each; 372GB / 16 TB/s per superchip per NVIDIA). Grace's up-to-480GB LPDDR5X (up to 512 GB/s) is cache-coherent over NVLink-C2C (900 GB/s) and addressable by the GPUs as slower 'extended GPU memory' — usable for offload but at ~1/16 the HBM bandwidth. GB200 NVL72 rack: 72 GPUs, 13.4TB HBM3e @ 576 TB/s + 17TB LPDDR5X.

devices.name: GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip
devices.usable_note: Aggregate of 2x Blackwell Ultra (B300) GPUs at 288GB raw each + 1 Grace CPU. NVIDIA's GB300 NVL72 page lists 20TB GPU memory across 72 GPUs (~278GB/GPU) and 17TB LPDDR5X CPU memory ('37TB fast memory' combined), implying the same provisioning haircut as B300 (~270GB exposed per GPU). Per-GPU HBM bandwidth up to 8 TB/s.

devices.name: DGX Spark (GB10 Grace Blackwell)
devices.usable_note: True UMA — no fixed CPU/GPU split and no sysctl-style carveout knob; the GPU can allocate any memory the kernel can free. Mechanics per NVIDIA docs: nvidia-smi shows 'Memory-Usage: Not Supported' (iGPU, no dedicated framebuffer); cudaMemGetInfo under-reports because it ignores reclaimable page cache/SWAP (workaround: read MemAvailable from /proc/meminfo, or 'echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches'). In practice: firmware+kernel reserve ~7GiB, so free -h shows ~121GiB total and deviceQuery reports ~122,570 MiB (~119.7GiB) total global GPU memory; field reports show ~94GB models loading with ~27GiB still available, i.e. ~110-120GB practically usable for weights+KV after the OS/desktop (~5-10GB). usable_fraction 0.9 reflects ~115GB of the marketed 128GB under a typical idle Ubuntu/DGX OS load.

research.vendor_notes: Memory-architecture facts affecting usable capacity: (1) HBM datacenter GPUs (A100/H100/H200/B200/B300) use sideband ECC — no capacity or bandwidth penalty, ECC always on; the nvidia-smi total is the full marketed figure (e.g. H100 SXM ~81.5GiB). (2) GDDR workstation/server boards (RTX A6000, RTX 6000 Ada, L40S, RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell) use inline ECC: when ECC is enabled (off by default on workstation cards, commonly on for L40S in servers), ~6.25% of VRAM is reserved for ECC bits and bandwidth drops slightly — a VRAM calculator should optionally subtract this when the user has ECC on (sources: https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/products/workstations/professional-desktop-gpus/rtx-pro-6000/, NVIDIA vGPU/ECC documentation). (3) Blackwell provisioning: B200 was announced at 192GB but ships exposing 180GB (Lenovo HGX B200 guide lp2226; DGX B200 = 1,440GB/8); B300 is marketed at 288GB but NVIDIA's DGX B300 page totals 2.1TB/8 GPUs (~270GB exposed), same ~6.25% haircut — use 180/270 as the allocatable numbers. (4) Grace superchips (GB200/GB300): HBM3e is GPU-local but Grace's LPDDR5X (480GB @ 512 GB/s per superchip) is cache-coherent via 900 GB/s NVLink-C2C and GPU-addressable as extended memory — usable for KV/weight offload at far lower bandwidth than HBM (https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72/). (5) DGX Spark is the only fully unified-memory device here: 128GB LPDDR5x shared CPU+GPU at 273 GB/s, no partition; firmware/kernel reserve ~7GiB (OS sees ~121GiB, deviceQuery ~122,570 MiB), and standard CUDA accounting tools misreport availability (cudaMemGetInfo ignores reclaimable page cache; nvidia-smi memory fields unsupported) — practical LLM budget is ~110-120GB (https://docs.nvidia.com/dgx/dgx-spark/known-issues.html). Bandwidth, not capacity, is its bottleneck (LMSYS review). Note also H100 NVL's '188GB' marketing figure is a 2-card NVLink pair (94GB each), and GB200/GB300 entries above are 2-GPU superchips — per-GPU figures are 186GB and 288GB(raw)/~270GB(exposed) respectively.

corrections.claim: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell — memory_bandwidth_gbs / usable_note ('1792 GB/s on all three editions')
corrections.was: 1792 GB/s claimed for Workstation 600W, Max-Q 300W, AND Server editions
corrections.should_be: 1792 GB/s applies only to Workstation and Max-Q editions. The Server Edition is 1597 GB/s per NVIDIA's own spec table and Lenovo's product guide. Either split the Server Edition into its own row (96GB GDDR7, 1597 GB/s) or fix the note.
corrections.source: https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/rtx-pro-6000-blackwell-server-edition/ (spec table: '96 GB GDDR7', '1597 GB/s'); https://lenovopress.lenovo.com/lp2263-thinksystem-nvidia-rtx-pro-6000-blackwell-server-edition-pcie-gen5-gpu ('Up to 1597 GB/s')

corrections.claim: B300 (Blackwell Ultra) — usable_fraction and usable_note exposed-capacity figure
corrections.was: usable_fraction 0.9375 (implies 270GB exposed); note says '~270GB exposed... same ~6.25% provisioning pattern as B200', citing verda.com/server-parts.eu third-party blogs
corrections.should_be: Exposed per-GPU capacity is 279GB (haircut is ~3.1%, not 6.25%; usable_fraction ≈ 0.969). CoreWeave's production GB300 NVL72 instance docs list 279 GB per Blackwell Ultra GPU; NVIDIA's GB300 NVL72 page total (20TB/72 ≈ 278GB) matches; the GB300 NVL72 datasheet footnote cited by multiple outlets says 279GB/chip. NVIDIA's technical blog and Azure's ND GB300-v6 VM table both list 288GB/GPU as the headline figure. The 270GB figure appears in no primary source. (NVIDIA's DGX/HGX B300 pages say '2.1TB total' = 262.5GB/GPU — an internal NVIDIA inconsistency; the deployed/exposed figure 279GB should win for a VRAM calculator.)
corrections.source: https://docs.coreweave.com/platform/instances/gpu/gb300-4x-e ('279 GB' per GPU); https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb300-nvl72/ ('20 TB' GPU memory / 72 GPUs); https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/inside-nvidia-blackwell-ultra-the-chip-powering-the-ai-factory-era/ (288 GB HBM3E, 8 TB/s); https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes/gpu-accelerated/nd-gb300-v6-series (288GB/accelerator)

corrections.claim: GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip — usable_fraction and usable_note
corrections.was: usable_fraction 0.9375 (implies 540GB usable of 576GB raw); note says '~270GB exposed per GPU'
corrections.should_be: ~558GB usable per superchip (2 x 279GB exposed per GPU); usable_fraction ≈ 0.969. Same correction as B300: the per-GPU exposed figure is 279GB, confirmed by CoreWeave GB300 NVL72 production instances and consistent with NVIDIA's 20TB/72-GPU rack total.

corrections.claim: vendor_notes — 'B300... same ~6.25% haircut — use 180/270 as the allocatable numbers'
corrections.was: Recommends 270GB as the B300 allocatable number, asserting the same 6.25% provisioning haircut as B200
corrections.should_be: Use 180GB (B200) and 279GB (B300) as allocatable numbers. B300's haircut is 288→279 (~3.1%), not 6.25%. The B200 192→180 part of the note is correct and was independently confirmed (nvidia-smi reports 183,359 MiB ≈ 179 GiB).

missing: GH200 Grace Hopper Superchip — major omission for a datacenter table: 96GB HBM3 (original) or 141GB HBM3e (GH200 NVL2 / late revision) GPU memory + 480GB LPDDR5X coherent over NVLink-C2C; widely deployed for LLM inference with the same extended-GPU-memory offload pattern the researcher describes for GB200 (https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/grace-hopper-superchip/)

DGX Station GB300 (Grace Blackwell Ultra Desktop Superchip) — 288GB HBM3e GPU + ~496GB LPDDR5X coherent CPU memory (~784GB total addressable); directly relevant to can-I-run-it users (https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/products/workstations/dgx-station/)
L4 — 24GB GDDR6, 300 GB/s; one of the most widely deployed inference GPUs (GCP G2 instances)
L40 (48GB GDDR6, 864 GB/s) and A40 (48GB GDDR6, 696 GB/s) — datacenter visualization/inference boards still common in clouds; both inline-ECC GDDR like L40S
A30 (24GB HBM2, 933 GB/s) and A10/A10G (24GB GDDR6, 600 GB/s) — A10G backs AWS g5 instances, very common for can-I-run-it questions
V100 (16GB/32GB HBM2, 900 GB/s) — legacy but still rentable on many clouds
H20 (China export variant) — 96GB HBM3 @ 4.0 TB/s, plus the later 141GB HBM3e version; A800/H800 export variants (memory specs same as A100 80GB / H100 80GB) — relevant to a global user base
RTX 5000 Ada (32GB GDDR6) and RTX PRO 5000 Blackwell (48GB GDDR7, plus the 72GB variant NVIDIA added in late 2025) — workstation cards adjacent to the included RTX 6000-class entries
H100 96GB SXM variant exists in some clouds (rare, but appears in fleet listings); optional
Vera Rubin (VR200 NVL144, HBM4) — announced at GTC 2025/2026 for late-2026 ship; worth listing as status 'announced' so users don't expect it to be runnable yet

MIG as a general capability note: A100/H100/H200/B200 all support MIG partitioning (e.g. H100 into 7x ~10GB instances), which changes per-instance usable VRAM — the table only mentions MIG for RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell
verification.summary: Re-verified 12 of 15 devices directly against primary sources (NVIDIA spec pages/datasheets, NVIDIA docs, Lenovo product guides, Azure VM specs, CoreWeave production instance docs). The dataset is largely accurate: A6000/RTX 6000 Ada/L40S/A100/H100 (SXM, PCIe, NVL)/H200/B200/GB200/DGX Spark figures all check out, including the surprising ones (B200 ships at 180GB not 192GB — confirmed via Lenovo lp2226, DGX B200 1,440GB total, and a dev-forum report of ~179 GiB physically available; H100 NVL 94GB/3.9TB/s; DGX Spark 273 GB/s with all the unified-memory accounting quirks confirmed on NVIDIA's known-issues page; GB200 at 186GB/GPU confirmed by CoreWeave production docs). Two real errors found: (1) RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition has 1597 GB/s bandwidth, not the 1792 GB/s the entry claims for 'all three editions' (NVIDIA's own Server Edition page + Lenovo lp2263); (2) the B300/GB300 'exposed ~270GB' claim is unsupported — production GB300 NVL72 deployments expose 279GB per GPU (CoreWeave docs; matches NVIDIA's 20TB/72-GPU rack total and the 279GB datasheet footnote), so the haircut is ~3.1% (usable_fraction ~0.969), not the B200-style 6.25% the researcher assumed; NVIDIA's DGX/HGX B300 '2.1TB' page figure is an internal inconsistency that should not drive the calculator. Biggest coverage gaps: GH200, DGX Station GB300, and the common inference fleet (L4, L40, A40, A30, A10G, V100) plus China export variants (H20/A800/H800).

devices.name: Apple M1 (2020)
devices.usable_note: macOS default GPU wired limit is ~2/3 of RAM on machines with <=32GB: 8GB -> ~5.33GB, 16GB -> ~10.67GB reported as Metal recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize. Override: sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=<MiB> (macOS 14+).

devices.name: Apple M1 Pro (2021)
devices.usable_note: Both factory configs are <=32GB, so default Metal cap is ~2/3 of RAM: 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 32GB -> ~21.33GB. Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb sysctl.

devices.name: Apple M1 Max (2021)
devices.usable_note: Config-dependent default Metal cap: 32GB config -> ~2/3 (~21.33GB); 64GB config -> 75% (48GB, recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 49152 MiB). Users on 64GB M1 Max commonly raise to 56GB via sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=57344.

devices.name: Apple M1 Ultra (2022)
devices.memory_type: LPDDR5-6400 unified (2x M1 Max dies, UltraFusion)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 64GB -> 48GB, 128GB -> 96GB (recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 98304 MiB). Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb (e.g. 122880 for 120GB on a 128GB machine).

devices.name: Apple M2 (2022)
devices.usable_note: All configs <=32GB -> default Metal cap ~2/3: 8GB -> ~5.33GB, 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 24GB -> 16GB. Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb sysctl.

devices.name: Apple M2 Pro (2023)
devices.usable_note: Both configs <=32GB -> default Metal cap ~2/3: 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 32GB -> ~21.33GB. Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb sysctl.

devices.name: Apple M2 Max (2023)
devices.usable_note: Config-dependent default Metal cap: 32GB -> ~2/3 (~21.33GB); 64GB -> 48GB and 96GB -> 72GB (75%). Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb sysctl.

devices.name: Apple M2 Ultra (2023)
devices.memory_type: LPDDR5-6400 unified (2x M2 Max dies, UltraFusion)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 64GB -> 48GB, 128GB -> 96GB, 192GB -> 144GB (recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 147456 MiB). Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb sysctl.

devices.name: Apple M3 (2023)
devices.usable_note: All configs <=32GB -> default Metal cap ~2/3: 8GB -> ~5.33GB, 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 24GB -> 16GB. Theoretical bandwidth 102.4GB/s (Apple markets 100GB/s).

devices.name: Apple M3 Pro (2023)
devices.usable_note: Config-dependent: 18GB config -> ~2/3 (12GB); 36GB config -> 75% (27GB). Theoretical bandwidth 153.6GB/s (Apple markets 150GB/s).

devices.name: Apple M3 Max, 14-core CPU / 30-core GPU (2023)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 36GB -> 27GB (empirically verified: llama.cpp on 36GB M3 Max logs recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 28991.03 MB decimal = exactly 27 GiB), 96GB -> 72GB. Theoretical 307.2GB/s (Apple markets 300GB/s).

devices.name: Apple M3 Max, 16-core CPU / 40-core GPU (2023)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 48GB -> 36GB, 64GB -> 48GB, 128GB -> 96GB. Theoretical 409.6GB/s (Apple markets 400GB/s). Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb.

devices.name: Apple M3 Ultra (2025, Mac Studio)
devices.memory_type: LPDDR5-6400 unified (1024-bit bus, 2x M3 Max dies)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 96GB -> 72GB, 256GB -> 192GB, 512GB -> 384GB. 512GB owners running DeepSeek-class models routinely raise the limit to ~448-500GB via sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb. Largest single-node unified pool Apple ships as of June 2026.

devices.name: Apple M4 (2024)
devices.usable_note: Mac configs (16/24/32GB) all <=32GB -> default Metal cap ~2/3: 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 24GB -> 16GB, 32GB -> ~21.33GB. Note: M4 iPad Pro has 8GB/16GB variants but iPadOS imposes much stricter per-app memory limits; calculator should target Macs.

devices.name: Apple M4 Pro (2024)
devices.usable_note: Config-dependent: 24GB -> ~2/3 (16GB); 48GB -> 36GB and 64GB -> 48GB (75%). 64GB option is Mac mini only; MacBook Pro M4 Pro tops out at 48GB. Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb.

devices.name: Apple M4 Max, 14-core CPU / 32-core GPU (2024)
devices.usable_note: 36GB -> 27GB default Metal cap (75%). Binned M4 Max ships only with 36GB at 410GB/s per Apple spec page.

devices.name: Apple M4 Max, 16-core CPU / 40-core GPU (2024)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 48GB -> 36GB, 64GB -> 48GB, 128GB -> 96GB. Override via iogpu.wired_limit_mb.

devices.name: Apple M5 (Oct 2025)
devices.usable_note: Mac configs (16/24/32GB, MacBook Pro 14-inch and MacBook Air) all <=32GB -> default Metal cap ~2/3: 16GB -> ~10.67GB, 24GB -> 16GB, 32GB -> ~21.33GB. A 12GB variant exists only in iPad Pro. Theoretical bandwidth 153.6GB/s (Apple markets 153GB/s).

devices.name: Apple M5 Pro (Mar 2026)
devices.usable_note: Config-dependent: 24GB -> ~2/3 (16GB); 48GB -> 36GB and 64GB -> 48GB (75%). Announced Mar 3 2026, available Mar 11 2026 in MacBook Pro 14/16-inch. Two-die Fusion Architecture but a single unified memory pool. CPU variants: 15-core (16-core GPU) and 18-core (20-core GPU); Apple lists 307GB/s for both.

devices.name: Apple M5 Max, 32-core GPU (Mar 2026)
devices.usable_note: 36GB -> 27GB default Metal cap (75%). Binned M5 Max (18-core CPU, 32-core GPU) ships with 36GB at 460GB/s per Apple spec page.

devices.name: Apple M5 Max, 40-core GPU (Mar 2026)
devices.usable_note: Default Metal cap 75%: 48GB -> 36GB, 64GB -> 48GB, 128GB -> 96GB; override via sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb. Highest-bandwidth Apple Silicon laptop chip as of June 2026 (614GB/s, up to 4x AI perf vs M4 generation per Apple).

devices.name: Apple M5 Ultra (unannounced/rumored)
devices.usable_note: DOES NOT EXIST YET. Not announced as of June 12, 2026 (no WWDC 2026 announcement confirmed in available sources). Rumored for a 2026 Mac Studio with up to ~256GB and ~1.2TB/s if 2x M5 Max scaling holds; Gurman reports RAM shortages may push launch to October 2026. Do not include in a shipping-hardware calculator; the 256GB figure is a rumor, not a spec.

research.vendor_notes: UNIFIED MEMORY + GPU CAP MECHANISM (the key deliverable): All Apple Silicon chips share one LPDDR pool between CPU/GPU/Neural Engine — there is no separate VRAM and no ECC reservation. macOS caps how much of that pool the GPU may wire down; Metal exposes the cap as MTLDevice.recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize, and despite the name it behaves as a HARD ceiling for Metal allocations (confirmed on Apple Developer Forums thread 732035; llama.cpp, Ollama, LM Studio and MLX all treat it as the effective VRAM size). DEFAULT FORMULA (empirically pinned this session): RAM <= 32GB -> ~2/3 of RAM (8GB->5.33, 16GB->10.67, 18GB->12, 24GB->16, 32GB->21.33GB; Ollama log on 32GB shows 22906.50 decimal MB = 21.33 GiB); RAM >= 36GB -> 75% (36GB->27 [llama.cpp issue #7060: 36GB M3 Max logs recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 28991.03 MB = exactly 27 GiB], 48->36, 64->48, 96->72, 128->96, 192->144, 256->192, 512->384GB). The boundary sits between 32GB and 36GB; no factory config exists in between, so per-SKU the rule is exact. Some blogs claim a 64GB threshold — that is wrong; the 36GB and 48GB data points prove 75% applies from 36GB up. OVERRIDE: macOS 14 Sonoma and later (incl. Sequoia 15 / Tahoe 26): sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=<MiB> — takes effect immediately, no reboot, no SIP change; value 0 restores default; resets on reboot unless persisted in /etc/sysctl.conf or a LaunchDaemon. macOS 13 Ventura used sudo sysctl debug.iogpu.wired_limit=<bytes>. Practical guidance from the llama.cpp/MLX community: leave 8-16GB (or ~10%) for macOS; pushing near 100% of RAM causes memory pressure, swapping, UI stalls or hangs. A VRAM calculator should default usable memory to 2/3 (<=32GB SKUs) or 75% (>=36GB SKUs) and optionally model the sysctl override as (RAM - ~8GB) for advanced users. Sources: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/metal/mtldevice/recommendedmaxworkingsetsize, https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/732035, https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/2182, https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues/7060, https://blog.peddals.com/en/fine-tune-vram-size-of-mac-for-llm/, https://github.com/ivanopcode/devnote-override-macos-metal-vram-cap, https://stencel.io/posts/apple-silicon-limitations-with-usage-on-local-llm%20.html. TIER EXISTENCE PER GENERATION (honest, as of June 12, 2026): M1 = Base/Pro/Max/Ultra (all shipped). M2 = Base/Pro/Max/Ultra (all shipped). M3 = Base/Pro/Max shipped 2023; M3 Ultra shipped March 2025 in Mac Studio. M4 = Base/Pro/Max shipped 2024; NO M4 ULTRA WAS EVER RELEASED — the 2025 Mac Studio pairs M4 Max with the M3 Ultra (https://www.apple.com/mac-studio/specs/). M5 = Base shipped Oct 15, 2025 (MacBook Pro 14", iPad Pro, Vision Pro; MacBook Air M5 followed); M5 Pro and M5 Max announced Mar 3, 2026 and shipping since Mar 11, 2026 in MacBook Pro (https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-debuts-m5-pro-and-m5-max-to-supercharge-the-most-demanding-pro-workflows/, https://support.apple.com/en-us/126318); M5 ULTRA IS NOT ANNOUNCED — rumored for a 2026 Mac Studio, possibly delayed to Oct 2026 by RAM shortages (https://www.macrumors.com/2025/11/04/mac-studio-m5-ultra-2026/). Binned Max chips have distinct memory buses and are split into separate entries: M3 Max 30-core GPU = 300GB/s (36/96GB) vs 40-core = 400GB/s (48/64/128GB); M4 Max 32-core GPU = 410GB/s (36GB only) vs 40-core = 546GB/s (48/64/128GB); M5 Max 32-core GPU = 460GB/s (36GB only) vs 40-core = 614GB/s (48/64/128GB). Apple marketing bandwidths are slightly rounded from theoretical (e.g. 153 vs 153.6, 400 vs 409.6, 819 vs 819.2GB/s). iPad Pro/Vision Pro variants of M-series chips carry different RAM (M4 iPad: 8/16GB; M5 iPad: 12/16GB) and iPadOS/visionOS enforce much stricter per-app memory limits than the macOS wired-limit mechanism — a Mac-focused calculator should ignore them.

Bandwidth is 819GB/s on both (verified at https://www.apple.com/mac-studio/specs/), so the single entry is fine for bandwidth, but a per-config calculator dropdown should not offer 512GB with the binned chip.

Source: https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-introduces-the-new-macbook-air-with-m5/

Binned M1 Pro (8-core CPU/14-core GPU) and binned M1 Max (24-core GPU) variants exist but share identical memory options and bandwidth with the full chips — omission is harmless, noted for completeness.
Mac Pro (2023) still tops out at M2 Ultra/192GB as of June 2026 — covered by the existing M2 Ultra entry; no separate row needed but worth a note if the calculator lists products rather than chips.
verification.summary: Adversarially re-verified the riskiest claims against primary sources, re-fetching Apple spec pages as raw HTML rather than trusting citations. Post-cutoff claims all check out: support.apple.com/en-us/126318 confirms M5 Pro (307GB/s; 15C/16GPU and 18C/20GPU variants; 24/48/64GB), M5 Max 32-core GPU (460GB/s, 36GB only) and 40-core GPU (614GB/s, 48/64/128GB), announced Mar 3 2026 / available Mar 11 2026 with two-die Fusion Architecture (Apple newsroom, MacRumors 2026/03/03). support.apple.com/en-us/125405 confirms M5 base 153GB/s and 16/24/32GB Mac configs. support.apple.com/en-us/121555 and /121553 confirm M4 120GB/s, M4 Pro 273GB/s with 64GB available only on Mac mini (MacBook Pro M4 Pro caps at 48GB), and the M4 Max 410/546GB/s binned/full split with 36GB-only on the binned chip. apple.com/mac-studio/specs confirms M3 Ultra 819GB/s on both GPU variants and that the 2025 Mac Studio pairs M4 Max with M3 Ultra (no M4 Ultra — researcher's negative claim is correct). The unified-memory cap mechanism is solid: llama.cpp issue #7060 confirms verbatim 'recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize = 28991.03 MB' (= 27GiB = 75%) on a 36GB M3 Max, validating the 2/3-below-32GB vs 75%-from-36GB boundary and debunking the 64GB-threshold myth as the researcher claimed. M5 Ultra non-existence confirmed: WWDC 2026 (June 8) produced no Mac Studio; launch now expected ~Oct 2026. M1/M2/M3 capacities, memory options, LPDDR types, and bandwidths (68.25/100/150/200/300/400/800/819 GB/s) all match primary-era documentation with no discrepancies. Only one correction: the rumored M5 Ultra memory placeholder (256GB) is stale — June 2026 rumors say up to 512GB — and it is in an entry already flagged as rumored/do-not-include. Dataset is reliable for use; verdict is minor-corrections solely for the rumor figure plus a few config-pairing nuances listed under missing.

devices.name: AMD Instinct MI300X
devices.usable_note: HBM ECC is sideband and does not consume visible capacity; rocm-smi exposes ~192 GB. Inference stacks (vLLM) typically self-cap at gpu-memory-utilization ~0.9, but that is app-level, not hardware. 8-GPU OAM platform = 1.5 TB aggregate.

devices.usable_note: Full 256 GB HBM3e exposed; ECC sideband. 8-GPU platform = 2 TB aggregate.

devices.name: AMD Instinct MI350X
devices.usable_note: CDNA4, 288 GB HBM3e at 8 TB/s; AMD markets running a 520B+ parameter model on one GPU. 8-GPU platform = 2.3 TB aggregate.

devices.name: AMD Instinct MI355X
devices.usable_note: Same 288 GB HBM3e / 8 TB/s as MI350X; higher-TBP liquid-cooled SKU with higher clocks. 8-GPU platform = 2.3 TB aggregate.

devices.name: AMD Instinct MI455X (MI400 series, CDNA5)

devices.name: AMD Instinct MI450 (MI400 series volume SKU)
devices.usable_note: Same 432 GB HBM4 / 19.6 TB/s as MI455X at lower power envelope. Other family members: MI430X/MI440X (HPC/FP64 variants). OpenAI (6 GW) and Meta (6 GW) deals start H2 2026.

devices.name: AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX
devices.usable_note: Full 24 GB allocatable as dedicated VRAM; small driver reservation (~hundreds of MB) only.

devices.name: AMD Radeon RX 7900 XT
devices.usable_note: 20 GB GDDR6 on 320-bit bus at 20 Gbps = 800 GB/s.

devices.name: AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT
devices.usable_note: RDNA4, 256-bit bus, 20.1 Gbps effective. AMD lists max bandwidth 644.6 GB/s.

devices.name: AMD Radeon AI PRO R9700
devices.usable_note: RDNA4 (Navi 48, same silicon as RX 9070 XT) with doubled 32 GB GDDR6 on a 256-bit bus at 20 Gbps; AMD datasheet says up to 640 GB/s (TechPowerUp lists 644.6 GB/s at 20.1 Gbps). The current go-to AMD prosumer local-LLM card.

devices.name: AMD Radeon PRO W7900
devices.usable_note: 48 GB ECC GDDR6, 384-bit at 18 Gbps = 864 GB/s. AMD advertises the full 48 GB with ECC; no documented capacity reduction when ECC is active. Also sold as W7900 Dual Slot ($3,499 AI-focused variant).

devices.name: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 (Strix Halo)
devices.usable_note: Mechanism: (1) Windows — AMD 'Variable Graphics Memory' in Adrenalin (or BIOS UMA frame buffer) carves out dedicated VRAM, max 96 GB on a 128 GB system (= 0.75); llama.cpp/LM Studio Vulkan users run with 96 GB. (2) Linux — GPU uses GTT (dynamically mapped system RAM), default ~50% of RAM; raise via ttm.pages_limit / amdgpu.gttsize kernel params or AMD's amd-ttm tool (e.g. 'amd-ttm --set 100' = 100 GB; community configs reach ~110-120 GB, i.e. up to ~0.94, leaving the rest for OS/CPU). ROCm docs recommend keeping BIOS dedicated VRAM tiny (512 MB) and relying on GTT. Kernel 6.16.9+/6.18.4+ improves UMA handling; some ROCm builds had a 64 GB allocation cap bug (fixed in stable ROCm 7.2.4).
devices.status: shipping (since H1 2025; Framework Desktop, HP ZBook Ultra G1a, mini-PCs)

devices.name: AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 series (Gorgon Halo)
devices.usable_note: AMD confirms up to 160 GB GPU-allocatable VRAM on 192 GB configs (160/192 = 0.833) via the same Variable Graphics Memory / GTT mechanisms as Strix Halo. Zen 5 + RDNA 3.5 refresh of Strix Halo; LPDDR5X-8533 gives ~273 GB/s.
devices.status: announced (CES Jan 2026; systems expected through 2026 — verify shipping before listing)

research.vendor_notes: Three memory-architecture regimes matter for a VRAM calculator. (1) Instinct datacenter HBM parts (MI300X 192GB HBM3 @5.3TB/s, MI325X 256GB HBM3e @6TB/s, MI350X/MI355X 288GB HBM3e @8TB/s): ECC is sideband on HBM so the full nominal capacity is exposed to ROCm (rocm-smi reports ~nominal); only a small firmware/driver reservation applies, and serving stacks like vLLM default to ~90% utilization as a software safety margin, not a hardware cap. Standard platforms are 8x OAM (1.5TB / 2TB / 2.3TB aggregate). The MI400 series (MI455X/MI450/MI430X/MI440X, CDNA5, 432GB HBM4 @19.6TB/s) was fully unveiled at CES Jan 2026 and is sampling, with production/Helios-rack (72 GPUs, 31TB HBM4) shipments stated as on-target for H2/Q3 2026 — i.e., announced, NOT generally shipping as of June 2026; specs are pre-production vendor figures. (2) Discrete consumer/prosumer GDDR6 cards (RX 7900 XTX 24GB @960GB/s, RX 7900 XT 20GB @800GB/s, RX 9070 XT 16GB @644.6GB/s, Radeon AI PRO R9700 32GB @640GB/s, Radeon PRO W7900 48GB ECC @864GB/s): effectively the full capacity is allocatable; no documented ECC capacity tax on the PRO cards. (3) Unified-memory APUs (Strix Halo / Ryzen AI Max+ 395, 32-128GB LPDDR5X-8000 256-bit = 256GB/s): GPU-usable memory is policy-limited, not hardware-limited. Windows: AMD Variable Graphics Memory caps dedicated carve-out at 96GB on 128GB systems (75%). Linux: GPU allocations are GTT-backed (default ~50% of RAM) and tunable via ttm.pages_limit/amdgpu.gttsize or AMD's amd-ttm utility — ROCm docs recommend 512MB BIOS VRAM + large GTT; community llama.cpp setups reach ~110-120GB of 128GB (~86-94%). Sum of (VRAM carve-out + GTT) minus OS needs is the real ceiling; calculators should default to 96GB (Windows) and offer a Linux override up to ~120GB. The announced Gorgon Halo (Ryzen AI Max 400) raises this to 192GB LPDDR5X-8533 (~273GB/s) with an AMD-confirmed 160GB GPU-allocatable cap. Sources: https://rocm.docs.amd.com/en/latest/how-to/system-optimization/strixhalo.html ; https://www.amd.com/en/products/accelerators/instinct/mi350/mi355x.html ; https://www.amd.com/content/dam/amd/en/documents/instinct-tech-docs/product-briefs/instinct-mi325x-datasheet.pdf ; https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/amd-denies-report-of-mi455x-delays-as-nvidia-vr200-systems-are-rumored-to-arrive-early-company-says-helios-systems-on-target-for-2h-2026 ; https://videocardz.com/newz/amd-confirms-ryzen-ai-max-400-gorgon-halo-will-support-up-to-192gb-memory-and-160gb-vram

corrections.should_be: MI400 series (432 GB HBM4, 19.6 TB/s) was first announced at AMD Advancing AI in June 2025; CES Jan 2026 was the full-lineup reveal (MI455X/MI440X/MI430X) and first hardware display. The substantive status claim (sampling, production H2 2026, NOT generally shipping as of June 2026) is correct and confirmed; only the announcement framing is off. Specs (432 GB HBM4, 19.6 TB/s, CDNA 5, Helios 72 GPUs = ~31 TB HBM4) re-verified against CES 2026 coverage.

corrections.claim: AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 (Gorgon Halo) — memory_options_gb [32, 64, 128, 192]
corrections.was: asserted option list 32/64/128/192 GB
corrections.should_be: Only 'up to 192 GB unified memory, up to 160 GB usable as VRAM (32 GB reserved for system)' is confirmed by AMD/Tom's; the intermediate config tiers (32/64/128) are extrapolated from Strix Halo and not yet confirmed for OEM Gorgon Halo systems (which ship starting Q3 2026 per AMD's blog). Also note AMD's official launch branding is 'Ryzen AI Max PRO 400 Series' (e.g. Ryzen AI Max+ PRO 495). The 160/192 = 0.833 usable_fraction and LPDDR5X-8533 (~273 GB/s on 256-bit) are confirmed.

corrections.claim: AMD Radeon AI PRO R9700 — memory_type 'GDDR6'
corrections.was: GDDR6 (no ECC mention)
corrections.should_be: GDDR6 with ECC support 'Yes (Linux Only)' per AMD's spec page — cosmetic for capacity purposes (no capacity tax), but worth noting since the W7900 entry does call out ECC. 32 GB / 640 GB/s figures confirmed correct.
corrections.source: https://www.amd.com/en/products/graphics/workstations/radeon-ai-pro/ai-9000-series/amd-radeon-ai-pro-r9700.html (fetched 2026-06-12: 'Dedicated Memory Size 32 GB ... Peak Memory Bandwidth 640 GB/s ... Memory ECC Support Yes (Linux Only)')

missing: AMD Instinct MI300A — the only AMD datacenter APU with true unified memory: 128 GB HBM3 @ 5.3 TB/s shared by CPU+GPU (El Capitan); highly relevant to a VRAM calculator's unified-memory category (https://www.amd.com/en/products/accelerators/instinct/mi300/mi300a.html)
AMD Instinct MI250X (128 GB HBM2e @ 3.28 TB/s) and MI210 (64 GB HBM2e @ 1.6 TB/s) — legacy CDNA2 parts still widely available in clusters/cloud and commonly asked about for can-I-run-it checks
AMD Radeon PRO W7800 (32 GB GDDR6 @ 576 GB/s) and W7800 48GB variant — sits between R9700 and W7900 in the workstation lineup
AMD Radeon RX 9060 XT 16 GB (128-bit GDDR6, ~322 GB/s, launched June 2025) — the budget local-LLM card most often paired with 8B-14B models; the 8 GB variant also exists
AMD Radeon RX 7800 XT (16 GB @ 624 GB/s) and RX 7600 XT (16 GB @ 288 GB/s) — popular 16 GB consumer options
AMD Radeon RX 9070 non-XT (16 GB @ 640 GB/s) and RX 9070 GRE (12 GB) — same Navi 48 family, only the XT was listed

AMD Ryzen AI 300 'Strix Point' / Ryzen AI 400 'Gorgon Point' standard APUs — 128-bit LPDDR5X (~120-137 GB/s) unified-memory laptops; far more common than Strix Halo and users will ask about them; usable VRAM follows the same VGM/GTT mechanisms

Windows-side GTT detail for Strix Halo: even without VGM, Windows allows GPU shared memory up to ~50% of system RAM on top of the dedicated carve-out — the researcher covers Linux GTT thoroughly but the Windows shared-memory pool is also relevant to ceiling calculations
verification.summary: Adversarially re-verified essentially every numeric claim against primary sources, fetching AMD's own spec pages directly (HTML spec tables extracted via curl since amd.com blocked the fetch tool). CONFIRMED EXACTLY: MI300X 192 GB HBM3 @ 5.3 TB/s; MI325X 256 GB HBM3E @ 6 TB/s; MI350X and MI355X 288 GB HBM3E @ 8 TB/s (all from amd.com spec tables, which also confirm full-chip ECC with no capacity reduction); RX 7900 XTX 24 GB @ 960 GB/s; RX 7900 XT 20 GB @ 800 GB/s; R9700 32 GB @ 640 GB/s; W7900 48 GB @ 864 GB/s 384-bit ECC. MI400-series claims (432 GB HBM4, 19.6 TB/s, CDNA 5, MI455X flagship + MI430X HPC + MI440X OAM + MI450 volume SKU, Helios 72-GPU/~31 TB rack, sampling now with production H2 2026 — not shipping as of June 2026) confirmed via CES 2026 coverage (ServeTheHome, Tom's, DCD). Strix Halo unified-memory mechanics fully confirmed against ROCm docs and AMD blogs: 96 GB max VGM on 128 GB Windows systems (0.75), Linux GTT default ~50% RAM, recommended 0.5 GB BIOS VRAM, amd-ttm helper (pipx amd-debug-tools), kernel 6.16.9 fix, and the 64 GB allocation cap bug in rocm7 nightlies with stable ROCm 7.2.4 recommended (kyuz0/amd-strix-halo-toolboxes README + ROCm GitHub issues 5444/5595). Gorgon Halo 192 GB / 160 GB VRAM (0.833) / LPDDR5X-8533 confirmed via AMD blog and Tom's; systems ship Q3 2026. ONE numeric correction: RX 9070 XT bandwidth — AMD officially lists 640 GB/s, not 644.6 GB/s (the researcher's note misattributes TechPowerUp's 20.1 Gbps effective figure to AMD). Minor nuances: MI400 was first announced June 2025 (CES 2026 was the full-lineup reveal), Gorgon Halo intermediate memory tiers are unconfirmed, and R9700 has Linux-only ECC. Notable misses: MI300A (128 GB unified HBM3 APU), MI250X/MI210 legacy parts, W7800, RX 9060 XT 16 GB, RX 7800 XT/7600 XT 16 GB, RX 9070 non-XT, and standard 128-bit Strix Point/Gorgon Point APUs. Overall the research output is reliable for table ingestion after the 9070 XT fix.

devices.name: Wormhole n150 (n150d active / n150s passive)
devices.usable_note: Dedicated on-card GDDR6, separate from host RAM; no OS/unified-memory cap. tt-metal allocator reserves only a small per-DRAM-bank barrier region; firmware/kernels reside in on-chip SRAM (108 MB), not GDDR6, so essentially all 12 GB is allocatable for tensors.

devices.name: Wormhole n300 (n300d active / n300s passive)
devices.usable_note: Two Wormhole ASICs on one card, 12 GB each (24 GB aggregate). Dedicated GDDR6, no OS cap. The two chips are separate memory domains linked over on-card Ethernet; a single tensor must fit/shard across the 12 GB per-chip pools, not a flat 24 GB. 192 MB SRAM total (96 MB/ASIC).

devices.name: Blackhole p100a
devices.usable_note: Harvested Blackhole part: 28 GB GDDR6 (vs 32 GB on full p150) and 448 GB/s. 120 Tensix cores, 180 MB SRAM. Dedicated GDDR6, no unified-memory/OS cap; full capacity allocatable minus small allocator barrier reservation. No QSFP networking ports.

devices.name: Blackhole p150a (active cooling, 4x QSFP-DD 800G)
devices.usable_note: Full Blackhole die: 32 GB GDDR6 @ 512 GB/s, 120 Tensix cores, 180 MB SRAM, 300W. Dedicated GDDR6, no OS cap; ~full 32 GB allocatable minus small allocator barrier region. p150a adds 4x QSFP-DD 800G ports for multi-card scale-out.

devices.name: Blackhole p150b (passive cooling, PCIe form factor)
devices.usable_note: Identical silicon/memory to p150a (32 GB GDDR6 @ 512 GB/s, 120 Tensix, 180 MB SRAM) but passive heatsink for rack servers. Dedicated GDDR6, no OS cap. (A p150c variant — same 32 GB — is used inside the Blackhole QuietBox; not sold standalone.)

devices.name: T3K / TT-LoudBox (Wormhole) — 4x n300s, 8 ASICs (TW-02002)
devices.usable_note: Canonical 'T3K' = TW-02002 with 4x n300s = 8 Wormhole ASICs = 96 GB GDDR6 aggregate, 192 MB SRAM, ~2.3 TB/s aggregate (8x288 GB/s). Memory is NOT a flat pool: 12 GB per chip across a 2x4 mesh, models shard over the 8 domains. A lower TW-02001 SKU uses 4x n150s = 4 ASICs = 48 GB. Host: 2x Intel Xeon Silver 4309Y + 256/512 GB DDR4 (separate from GDDR6).

devices.name: TT-QuietBox (Wormhole) — 4x n300, 8 ASICs
devices.usable_note: Liquid-cooled desktop: 4x Wormhole n300 = 8 ASICs = 96 GB GDDR6 aggregate (12 GB/chip, sharded across 8 domains), 192 MB SRAM. Host: AMD EPYC 8124P + 512 GB DDR5-4800 ECC (system RAM, separate from the 96 GB GDDR6). No OS cap on GDDR6.

devices.name: TT-QuietBox (Blackhole) — 'QB2' — 4x p150c
devices.usable_note: User's 'QB2'. Spec page confirms 4x Blackhole p150c accelerators; per-chip 32 GB -> 128 GB GDDR6 aggregate (~2.0 TB/s = 4x512 GB/s). 32 GB per chip, sharded across 4 domains, not a flat 128 GB pool. Host: AMD EPYC 8124P + 512 GB DDR5-4800 ECC (separate system RAM). Aggregate GDDR6 is arithmetic from confirmed 4x p150c x 32 GB.

devices.name: TT-LoudBox (Blackhole) — 8x p150b
devices.usable_note: Spec page confirms 8x Blackhole p150b; per-chip 32 GB -> 256 GB GDDR6 aggregate (~4.1 TB/s = 8x512 GB/s), sharded across 8 chip domains. Host: 2x AMD EPYC 9124 + 768 GB DDR5 ECC (separate system RAM). Aggregate GDDR6 is arithmetic from confirmed 8x p150b x 32 GB.

devices.name: Tenstorrent Galaxy (Wormhole) — 32 ASICs, 6U
devices.usable_note: 32 Wormhole Tensix processors x 12 GB = 384 GB globally-accessible GDDR6, ~3.8 GB on-die SRAM aggregate. Bandwidth ~9.2 TB/s if using the productized 288 GB/s/chip; some Tenstorrent materials cite a full 384 GB/s/chip Wormhole bus (~12.3 TB/s) — per-chip bandwidth in this server config is not pinned down, treat aggregate BW as approximate. Memory pooled over on-chip Ethernet mesh, not flat.

devices.name: Tenstorrent Galaxy (Blackhole) — 32 ASICs, 6U
devices.usable_note: Officially '1 TB of GDDR6' = 32 Blackhole x 32 GB = 1024 GB, 16 TB/s aggregate memory bandwidth (32x512 GB/s), 6.2 GB on-die SRAM @ 2.9 PB/s, 23 PFLOPS dense FP8. Memory pooled over a 100 Tbps Ethernet mesh (not a flat 1 TB pool). GA announced May 1, 2026; ~$110,000.

research.vendor_notes: All Tenstorrent accelerators use DEDICATED GDDR6 on the card/chip — it is NOT unified with the host CPU. Host systems carry separate DDR4/DDR5 system RAM (e.g. QuietBox = 512 GB DDR5, Blackhole LoudBox = 768 GB DDR5) that is NOT part of the GDDR6 accelerator memory and should not be counted as model VRAM. Per-chip memory: Wormhole = 12 GB GDDR6 @ 288 GB/s (full die spec sometimes cited at 384 GB/s; productized n150/n300 = 288 GB/s/chip); Blackhole = 32 GB GDDR6 @ 512 GB/s on full p150, harvested to 28 GB @ 448 GB/s on p100a. Card/system aggregates: n300 = 2 chips/24 GB; T3K (TT-LoudBox Wormhole, 4x n300s) and Wormhole QuietBox = 8 chips/96 GB; Blackhole QuietBox ('QB2', 4x p150c) = 4 chips/128 GB; Blackhole LoudBox (8x p150b) = 8 chips/256 GB; Galaxy Wormhole = 32 chips/384 GB; Galaxy Blackhole = 32 chips/1024 GB (1 TB). USABLE FRACTION: there is NO unified-memory/OS-style cap (this is not Apple/Metal recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize territory). The tt-metal allocator reserves only a small per-DRAM-bank 'barrier' region; firmware, kernels and runtime metadata live in on-chip SRAM (108 MB Wormhole / 180 MB Blackhole per chip), NOT in GDDR6 — so effectively the full GDDR6 capacity is allocatable for tensors, minus a small (KB-to-MB-scale, not publicly quantified) reservation. The real practical constraint for multi-chip SKUs is topology, not a usable percentage: GDDR6 is distributed per-chip (12 GB Wormhole / 32 GB Blackhole) and pooled over an on-chip Ethernet mesh, so a model and its activations must be sharded across the per-chip pools rather than treated as one flat aggregate buffer. Naming note: 'T3K' = the 8-Wormhole-chip config = TT-LoudBox TW-02002 (4x n300s); a smaller TW-02001 uses 4x n150s = 48 GB. 'p150c' is a system-integration-only Blackhole variant (same 32 GB) used in the Blackhole QuietBox and is not listed as a standalone retail card. Galaxy Blackhole reached GA May 1, 2026; Galaxy Wormhole and all Wormhole/Blackhole PCIe cards and QuietBox/LoudBox systems are shipping as of June 2026.

corrections.was: 23 PFLOPS dense FP8
corrections.should_be: 23 PFLOPS Block FP8 (block floating-point format, not 'dense')

corrections.claim: Galaxy Wormhole chassis form factor
corrections.was: 6U
corrections.should_be: Galaxy Wormhole is reported as a 4U server chassis (32 Wormhole chips in 4U); the '6U' label appears to be carried over from/confused with the Blackhole generation. Not load-bearing for VRAM.

missing: Grayskull e75 / e150 (prior-gen Tenstorrent PCIe accelerator, 8 GB GDDR6) is absent. It is EOL/discontinued so omission is defensible, but it is the only other named retail Tenstorrent accelerator a user might ask about.
Galaxy Wormhole list price (~$70,000 starting) and Galaxy Blackhole supercluster pricing ($440,000 for 4 Galaxies) are not captured — not VRAM-relevant but useful context.
verification.summary: Re-verified all 12 device entries against primary Tenstorrent sources (docs.tenstorrent.com spec pages, tenstorrent.com hardware pages, and the official Galaxy page/press). ALL VRAM-relevant numbers are correct: n150 = 12 GB GDDR6 @ 288 GB/s, 72 Tensix, 108 MB SRAM, 1 ASIC, 160W; n300 = 24 GB (2x12 GB ASICs) @ 576 GB/s, 192 MB SRAM (96/ASIC), 2 ASICs, 300W; Blackhole p100a = 28 GB @ 448 GB/s, 120 Tensix, 180 MB SRAM (no QSFP); p150a/p150b = 32 GB @ 512 GB/s, 120 Tensix, 180 MB SRAM, 300W, 4x QSFP-DD 800G (a=active, b=passive cooling). System aggregates verified: T3K/TT-LoudBox TW-02002 = 4x n300s = 8 ASICs = 96 GB (TW-02001 = 4x n150s = 4 ASICs = 48 GB); Wormhole QuietBox = 4x n300 = 8 ASICs = 96 GB (note: a WebFetch helper misread the doc as '4 ASICs' but n300 has 2 ASICs each, so 8 total / 96 GB is correct); Blackhole QuietBox confirmed 4x p150c = 128 GB; Blackhole LoudBox confirmed 8x p150b = 256 GB; Galaxy Wormhole = 32 chips = 384 GB (3.8 GB pooled SRAM, 9.32 PFLOPS FP8); Galaxy Blackhole = 32 chips = 1 TB GDDR6 @ 16 TB/s, 6.2 GB SRAM @ 2.9 PB/s, 23 PFLOPS Block FP8, $110,000 list, GA May 1 2026. Bandwidth aggregates (2304/2048/4096/9216/16384 GB/s) are correct arithmetic; researcher appropriately flagged Galaxy Wormhole BW as approximate. The dedicated-GDDR6 / no-unified-cap / per-chip-sharding narrative and the SRAM-holds-firmware claim are architecturally sound and consistent with cited tt-metal allocator docs. Only two nits found, neither affecting can-I-run-it verdicts: (1) Galaxy Blackhole FP8 is 'Block FP8' not 'dense FP8'; (2) Galaxy Wormhole is likely 4U, not 6U. Core memory data is reliable.

frameworks.name: vLLM
frameworks.kv_strategy: PagedAttention: KV cache stored in fixed-size blocks (default block_size=16 tokens, DEFAULT_BLOCK_SIZE=16 in vllm/config/cache.py; user-settable, platform-dependent choices). The entire KV pool is allocated at startup as one static tensor sized from a memory profiling run; blocks are then mapped to sequences via a block table. Internal fragmentation is bounded to the last partially-filled block per sequence (worst case block_size-1 tokens per seq), giving <4% waste vs 60-80% waste from fragmentation/over-reservation in naive contiguous allocators (vLLM blog/SOSP paper). V1 adds a hash-based prefix cache layered on the same block pool (parent-hash + token-hash per block, LRU eviction from the free queue), so prefix caching adds zero extra memory overhead — it only changes which free blocks get reused.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: kv_cache_dtype='auto' = follows model dtype (fp16/bf16). FP8 is NOT auto-enabled on Hopper — it must be requested explicitly. Without calibration, fp8 KV scales default to 1.0; dataset-calibrated scales via llm-compressor are recommended for accuracy.
frameworks.hardware_support: NVIDIA CUDA (primary; most quant kernels need Turing+, FP8 W8A8 needs Ada/Hopper+), AMD ROCm, Intel XPU, CPU (x86, ARM AArch64, Apple Silicon, IBM Z/S390X); via hardware-plugin system: Google TPU, AWS Neuron, Intel Gaudi, Huawei Ascend, IBM Spyre, others; Apple-GPU via community vLLM-Metal. (docs.vllm.ai installation index)

memory_overhead.description: total = weights + profiled peak activation + non-torch memory (CUDA context, NCCL buffers, allocator slack) + CUDA-graph pool + KV cache. Startup profiling measures the first three with a dummy forward pass at max_num_batched_tokens, then KV cache gets the remainder: available_kv = gpu_memory_utilization*total_vram - weights - peak_activation - non_torch - cudagraph_estimate (vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py; current main deducts a CUDA-graph estimate controlled by VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS; older releases did NOT, which is why users left 5-10% headroom outside gpu_memory_utilization). Real log examples (PR #10511 era): 8B model: weights 14.96 GiB, non_torch 0.18 GiB, activation 1.26 GiB; 27B: weights 27.59 GiB, non_torch 0.09 GiB, activation 1.48 GiB; 1.5B-class: weights 2.49 GiB, non_torch 0.02 GiB, activation 0.51 GiB. CUDA graphs add 'hundreds of MiB to several GiB' on top (vLLM forum); practitioners use a flat 1.5-2 GB estimate; default cudagraph_mode FULL_AND_PIECEWISE is the most memory-hungry; large/MoE models can OOM from graph capture alone (issue #38486: 33.4 GiB weights on 44.4 GiB GPU failed a 1.03 GiB allocation during capture). MoE imbalance and CUDA graphs are the two known sources of profiling inaccuracy (RFC #27951).
memory_overhead.activation_model: Profiled empirically at startup, not formulaic. Scales ~linearly with max_num_batched_tokens (defaults: 8192 API-server / 16384 LLM-class on >=70GB non-A100 GPUs; 2048/8192 otherwise; per vllm/engine/arg_utils.py) and with hidden_size and dtype. Rough calculator model: 0.5-1.5 GiB for <=8B dense models at default settings, 1.5-3 GiB for 27B-70B, more for MoE; chunked prefill (default on) caps the peak at max_num_batched_tokens tokens regardless of prompt length. Add ~0.5-3 GiB for CUDA-graph pool unless enforce_eager=True. Treat 'baseline_gb=2' (non-torch ~0.1-1 GiB + CUDA graphs ~1-2 GiB) as a rough single-GPU default; TP adds NCCL buffers (~0.5-1 GiB/GPU, rough).

frameworks.preallocates: YES — defining behavior. vLLM claims gpu_memory_utilization fraction of total VRAM per instance (default 0.92 on current main, vllm/config/cache.py Field(default=0.92); was 0.9 in all releases up to ~early 2026 — pick per targeted version) and allocates the ENTIRE KV block pool as a static tensor at startup. 'Required VRAM' for vLLM = gpu_memory_utilization x total_vram whenever weights+overhead fit; actual occupancy does not shrink with low load. Historically CUDA-graph capture consumed memory OUTSIDE this budget (users inverted semantics: set 0.7 to 'reserve 30% for graphs/activations', RFC #27951); current main folds a graph estimate into the budget. Alternative: kv_cache_memory_bytes sets an explicit KV budget and ignores gpu_memory_utilization. A calculator should report both 'minimum feasible VRAM' (weights+activation+overhead+minimal KV) and 'allocated VRAM' (= utilization x total).
quant_support: GPTQ (GPTQModel; broad hardware support incl. Volta-class via fallback kernels)
NVFP4 / NVIDIA Model Optimizer (modelopt; Blackwell FP4)

savings_techniques.saving: KV internal waste <4% (only last block per sequence, <=15 of 16 tokens worst case) vs 60-80% memory wasted by fragmentation/over-reservation in naive contiguous max-length allocation; for context, one 2K-token sequence on LLaMA-13B = ~1.7 GB KV

savings_techniques.technique: KV block sharing (parallel sampling / beam search)
savings_techniques.saving: up to 55% KV memory reduction for parallel-sampling/beam-search workloads via copy-on-write shared prefix blocks (-> up to 2.2x throughput)

exactly 50% KV bytes vs fp16/bf16 (1 byte vs 2 per element) -> ~2x more cacheable tokens in the same budget; near-lossless when scales are calibrated via llm-compressor; no waste-percentage change, pure dtype halving

savings_techniques.technique: 4-bit KV cache (nvfp4, turboquant_4bit_nc, turboquant_k8v4=8-bit K/4-bit V, 3-bit variants)
savings_techniques.saving: ~4x KV reduction vs 16-bit for nvfp4/4-bit variants, ~2.7x for k8v4 mixed, ~5x for 3-bit (rough; small overhead for per-group scales); newer kv_cache_dtype choices on current main, hardware-dependent

savings_techniques.technique: Automatic prefix caching (enable_prefix_caching, default True in V1)
savings_techniques.saving: ZERO additional VRAM cost and ZERO peak-VRAM saving — reuses blocks already inside the preallocated KV pool (LRU over the free queue); saves prefill COMPUTE/TTFT only. A VRAM calculator should model it as memory-neutral

savings_techniques.technique: Chunked prefill (enable_chunked_prefill, default True in V1)
savings_techniques.saving: caps peak activation memory at max_num_batched_tokens (default 2048-16384 by GPU/usage context) instead of full prompt length — e.g. a 128K-token prompt's activations are bounded to an 8K-token chunk, roughly a 16x activation-peak reduction for that case (rough, arithmetic)

savings_techniques.technique: Disable/limit CUDA graphs (enforce_eager=True or cudagraph_capture_sizes=[...] or cudagraph_mode)
savings_techniques.saving: recovers the CUDA-graph pool: 'hundreds of MiB to several GiB' depending on capture-size count and model (rough practitioner flat estimate 1.5-2 GB); default FULL_AND_PIECEWISE mode is highest-memory; trade-off: large decode-throughput loss (issue #38486 measured 14.4 tok/s eager vs 110 tok/s with graphs on a quantized model)

savings_techniques.technique: Weight quantization (AWQ/GPTQ/INT4 ~4x, FP8/INT8 ~2x vs fp16; GGUF various; NVFP4 ~4x)
savings_techniques.saving: weights shrink by the bit-width ratio (rough: 4-bit -> ~0.55-0.6 GB per B params incl. quant scales vs 2 GB/B at fp16); every GB saved goes directly to the KV pool because of the fixed gpu_memory_utilization budget
VRAM saving equal to the GiB value set (weights streamed from CPU per forward pass); severe bandwidth-bound slowdown; KV offload separately via kv_offloading_size (GiB buffer, default None)

savings_techniques.technique: Lower gpu_memory_utilization / explicit kv_cache_memory_bytes / max_model_len / max_num_seqs
savings_techniques.saving: directly shrinks the preallocated footprint: allocated VRAM = utilization x total_vram, so 0.5 vs 0.92 on an 80 GB GPU frees ~33.6 GB for co-tenants (arithmetic); kv_cache_memory_bytes pins KV exactly and ignores utilization; max_model_len/max_num_seqs reduce minimum-KV feasibility requirements, not the preallocated pool size
weights and KV pool split ~1/N per GPU (TP=2 halves per-GPU weights, rough since norms/embeddings replicate), at the cost of ~0.5-1 GiB/GPU extra NCCL/non-torch overhead (rough)

frameworks.notes: Version caveats for the calculator (verified against main branch, June 2026): gpu_memory_utilization default changed 0.9 -> 0.92 on main (docs dated Mar 2026 still say 0.9 for stable releases); calculate_kv_scales is deprecated, removal slated for v0.19; kv_cache_dtype option list grew substantially (nvfp4, turboquant, fp8_ds_mla). swap_space (default 4 GiB) and mm_processor_cache_gb (default 4 GiB) are CPU-RAM, not VRAM — exclude from VRAM math. Scheduler base defaults: max_num_batched_tokens=2048, max_num_seqs=128, but overridden per usage context/hardware (H100/MI300-class: 8192 tokens + 1024 seqs for API server, 16384 + 1024 for LLM class). Known modeling risk: vLLM's own profiling is acknowledged inaccurate for MoE models and CUDA graphs (RFC #27951), so add ~1-2 GiB safety margin (rough). Minimum-VRAM formula: weights + profiled_activation(~max_num_batched_tokens) + non_torch(0.1-1 GiB) + cudagraphs(0.5-3 GiB or 0 if eager) + KV(max_model_len worth of blocks at chosen kv dtype, +<4% block waste); Allocated-VRAM = gpu_memory_utilization x total.

corrections.claim: gpu_memory_utilization default — 'default 0.92 on current main ... was 0.9 in all releases up to ~early 2026'
corrections.was: 0.92 framed as a main-branch-only change, with stable releases implied to still be 0.9 (docs 'dated Mar 2026 still say 0.9 for stable releases')
corrections.should_be: 0.92 is in STABLE releases since v0.20.0 (released 2026-04-27). Verified per-tag: v0.18.0 and v0.19.0 = Field(default=0.9); v0.20.0, v0.21.0, v0.22.0, v0.22.1 (latest, 2026-06-05) = Field(default=0.92). A calculator should use 0.92 for vLLM >= 0.20 and 0.9 for <= 0.19, not 'main vs releases'

It is absent from vllm/config/cache.py and vllm/engine/arg_utils.py on main and on every checked tag v0.19.0 through v0.22.1 (grep count 0). It was a V0-era option; a calculator targeting current vLLM should not list it at all (the 'exclude from VRAM' conclusion was harmless but the option is gone)

corrections.should_be: True for almost all models, but cache.py on main and v0.22.1 documents an exception: 'Some models (namely DeepSeekV3.2) default to fp8' under auto (user must set bfloat16 to opt out). A KV-bytes calculator using auto=2 bytes would be 2x off for DeepSeek V3.2

corrections.claim: CUDA-graph estimate deduction 'current main deducts ... older releases did NOT'

corrections.should_be: Env var exists and defaults to enabled ('1'), and per the envs.py comment it has been 'Enabled by default as of v0.21.0' (released 2026-05-15). So stable v0.21+ already deducts the cudagraph estimate inside the gpu_memory_utilization budget; the boundary is v0.21.0, not main-vs-release
corrections.source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/main/vllm/envs.py (VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS default '1', comment 'Enabled by default as of v0.21.0') and https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/main/vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py determine_available_memory()

corrections.claim: 4-bit KV variants described as 'newer kv_cache_dtype choices on current main'
corrections.was: nvfp4/turboquant_* implied to be main-only
corrections.should_be: The full 15-option CacheDType list (incl. nvfp4, turboquant_k8v4, turboquant_4bit_nc, turboquant_k3v4_nc, turboquant_3bit_nc, fp8_ds_mla, int8/fp8_per_token_head) is identical in stable v0.22.1 — available in current releases, not just main

corrections.claim: memory_overhead example logs '(PR #10511 era): ... 27B: weights 27.59 GiB ... 1.5B-class: weights 2.49 GiB'
corrections.was: 27B and 1.5B-class log examples attributed to PR #10511
corrections.should_be: Only the 8B example (weights 14.96 GiB, non_torch 0.18 GiB, activation 1.26 GiB on an 80GB GPU) is verifiable in PR #10511 comments (matches exactly). The '27.59 GiB' and '2.49/0.51 GiB' figures do not appear in that PR; GitHub search traces 27.59GiB to issue #16692 (DeepSeek-R1 TP=16 per-GPU shard) and 2.49GiB+0.51GiB to issue #24578. Also note 27.59 GiB weights is implausible for a dense 27B bf16 model (~50+ GiB expected) — the '27B' label is wrong or refers to a quantized/sharded case; do not use it to calibrate an activation-vs-size table
corrections.confidence: high for the attribution error; the numbers themselves exist in other issues

corrections.claim: notes: 'calculate_kv_scales is deprecated, removal slated for v0.19'
corrections.was: removal slated for v0.19 reported as forward-looking fact
corrections.should_be: The docstring does say 'will be removed in v0.19', but v0.19.0 shipped 2026-04-03 and the option is STILL present in v0.22.1 and main — the removal note in the source is stale. Report as 'deprecated, removal note stale, still functional through v0.22.1'

corrections.claim: savings_techniques / CUDA graphs trade-off: 'issue #38486 measured 14.4 tok/s eager vs 110 tok/s with graphs on a quantized model'
corrections.was: framed as a same-model eager-vs-graphs comparison
corrections.should_be: Numbers are real but cross-model: 14.43 tok/s was Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8 with --enforce-eager; 109.97 tok/s was the GPTQ-Int4 variant of the same model WITH graphs (FP8+graphs OOMed, which was the bug). Directionally fine for 'graphs matter', but not a clean apples-to-apples 7.6x

missing: Sleep mode (LLM.sleep()/wake_up(), levels 1-2): level 1 offloads weights to CPU and discards KV cache, level 2 also discards weights — frees nearly the whole preallocated footprint between workloads (RLHF/co-tenancy use case); implemented in vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py sleep(). Directly relevant to a calculator's 'allocated VRAM does not shrink with low load' framing since this is the sanctioned way to make it shrink.

Hybrid/Mamba state-cache memory: CacheConfig on main has mamba_cache_dtype / mamba_ssm_cache_dtype (default 'auto', float32 ssm state common), mamba_block_size, mamba_cache_mode, mamba_page_size_padded — for hybrid models (Jamba, Granite-4, Qwen3-Next, Nemotron-H) the per-sequence SSM/conv state is a separate, often-float32 memory component the researcher's KV-only model misses.
kv_cache_dtype_skip_layers (list of layer patterns, e.g. 'sliding_window', exempt from KV quantization) — changes effective bytes-per-token when mixing fp8 KV with skipped layers.

Multimodal encoder cache: SchedulerConfig sets encoder_cache_size = max_num_batched_tokens (GPU-resident embedding cache for VLM models) — extra VRAM component for multimodal models, distinct from mm_processor_cache_gb (which is CPU).

LoRA memory: static buffers sized by max_loras x max_lora_rank are captured in CUDA graphs (scheduler.py compute_hash notes 'LoRA creates static buffers based on max_num_batched_tokens'), and cudagraph_specialize_lora=True doubles some graph captures — adds VRAM when serving adapters.
Speculative decoding: draft model weights + draft-token KV add VRAM; absent from the overhead model.

Per-usage-context max_num_seqs for <70GB GPUs is 256 (not the base 128) for both LLM class and API server, and TPU (V6E/V5E/V5P: 2048/1024/512 LLM-class) and CPU (4096*world_size) have their own max_num_batched_tokens defaults — researcher only gave the >=70GB-non-A100 and base values.
hash_block_size and prefix_caching_hash_algo (sha256 default, xxhash option) — metadata-only/no VRAM impact, but a completeness gap if the calculator documents prefix caching.

Version context worth stating outright: latest stable is v0.22.1 (2026-06-05); per-version defaults matrix should key off v0.20.0 (0.92 utilization) and v0.21.0 (cudagraph estimate folded into budget).
verification.summary: Re-verified the riskiest vLLM claims directly against primary sources: vllm/config/cache.py, scheduler.py, compilation.py, multimodal.py, offload.py, envs.py, engine/arg_utils.py, v1/worker/gpu_worker.py (main + tags v0.18.0-v0.22.1), the 2023 vLLM blog, GitHub PR #10511, issues #38486/#27951, and the discuss.vllm.ai CUDA-graph thread. CONFIRMED: DEFAULT_BLOCK_SIZE=16; all 15 kv_cache_dtype options exactly as listed; kv_cache_memory_bytes overrides/ignores gpu_memory_utilization; kv_offloading_size (GiB, default None, native/lmcache backends); enable_prefix_caching default True; cpu_offload_gb default 0 (UVA-based, now in offload.py); mm_processor_cache_gb default 4 GiB (CPU-side, duplicated per api_server+DP process); cudagraph_mode default FULL_AND_PIECEWISE; scheduler defaults 2048 tokens/128 seqs base, 16384-LLM/8192-API + 1024 seqs on >=70GB non-A100, 8192/2048 otherwise; profiling formula in determine_available_memory matches (weights + torch_peak + non_torch, cudagraph estimate gated by VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS); blog numbers (60-80% waste, <4%, 55%, 2.2x, 1.7GB LLaMA-13B) exact; issue #38486 numbers exact; RFC #27951 is real and says MoE imbalance + CUDA graphs break profiling; forum 'hundreds of MiB to several GiB' + 1.5-2GB flat estimate confirmed; PR #10511 8B log example (14.96/0.18/1.26 GiB) matches verbatim. CORRECTIONS (8, all minor but several affect calculator defaults): 0.92 utilization is stable since v0.20.0 (2026-04-27), not main-only; swap_space no longer exists in v0.19+; DeepSeekV3.2 defaults KV to fp8 under 'auto'; cudagraph-estimate deduction is default-on since stable v0.21.0; nvfp4/turboquant dtypes are in stable v0.22.1 too; the 27B (27.59 GiB — implausible for dense bf16 27B) and 1.5B log examples are misattributed to PR #10511; calculate_kv_scales 'removed in v0.19' note is stale (still present in v0.22.1); the #38486 eager-vs-graphs throughput comparison is across FP8 vs Int4 variants, not same-model. MISSED: sleep mode, Mamba/hybrid state-cache configs, multimodal encoder cache (GPU), kv_cache_dtype_skip_layers, LoRA buffers, speculative decoding, <70GB max_num_seqs=256 and TPU/CPU batch defaults. Overall the research is high quality and source-grounded; apply the version-boundary corrections (v0.20.0/v0.21.0) before loading into the data table.

frameworks.name: SGLang
frameworks.kv_strategy: Token-level paged KV cache managed by RadixAttention. Default --page-size is 1 (one token per page), so there is effectively zero internal block fragmentation, unlike block-paged designs (vLLM default block=16).
