Architecture is a two-level pool: a req_to_token mapping plus a single token_to_kv_pool tensor preallocated at startup; the RadixAttention radix tree maps token-sequence prefixes to KV tensors INSIDE that same pool, so prefix caching costs no extra VRAM — cached-but-unreferenced tokens are 'evictable' and reclaimed via recursive LRU eviction of leaf nodes when new requests need space. Cache-aware scheduling (longest-shared-prefix-first) raises hit rate. Note: some attention backends force larger page sizes (FlashMLA=64, Cutlass MLA=128, FA4 MHA=128, TRTLLM MHA in {16,32,64}); FlashInfer/FA3/Triton accept flexible/1. MLA models (DeepSeek) get a compressed MLATokenToKVPool sized by (kv_lora_rank + qk_rope_head_dim) per layer per token instead of 2*kv_heads*head_dim. HiCache (since ~Sep 2025) extends the radix tree hierarchically: GPU = L1, host RAM = L2, distributed storage (3FS/Mooncake/NIXL/file) = L3, with write-through/write-back policies and layer-wise prefetch overlap.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: auto = follows model weight dtype (so FP16/BF16 for typical checkpoints; 2 bytes/element). Exception in current main: DeepSeek DSA models on SM100+ hardware auto-resolve to fp8_e4m3. Options via --kv-cache-dtype: auto, fp8_e5m2, fp8_e4m3, bf16/bfloat16, fp4_e2m1 (FP4 KV is new and backend-restricted: FA4, Triton, FlashMLA, Cutlass MLA, TRTLLM support it; FlashInfer MHA does not).
frameworks.preallocates: YES — at startup SGLang allocates the KV pool as one contiguous tensor sized so that (model weights + KV pool) = mem_fraction_static * total GPU memory. CRITICAL semantic difference vs vLLM: vLLM's gpu_memory_utilization caps TOTAL usage (weights+KV+activations); SGLang's mem_fraction_static covers ONLY weights+KV pool — activations, CUDA graph buffers, and CUDA context land ON TOP of that fraction. So a server with mem_fraction_static=0.9 typically sits near 100% GPU utilization. Default mem_fraction_static: dynamically computed on current main as (gpu_mem - reserved_mem)/gpu_mem where reserved_mem [MB] = 512 + max(chunked_prefill_size,2048)*1.5 + cuda_graph_max_bs*2 + tp_size*pp_size/8*1024 (plus DP-attention extras); falls back to 0.88 if GPU memory can't be probed (older releases used static tiers ~0.88 TP1 down to ~0.79-0.81 TP8/16). For a VRAM calculator: 'required VRAM' = weights + desired KV pool + ~5-8 GB dynamic overhead; KV capacity in tokens = (mem_fraction_static*VRAM - weights) / bytes_per_token, with bytes_per_token = 2*num_layers*num_kv_heads*head_dim*dtype_size (MHA) or num_layers*(kv_lora_rank+qk_rope_head_dim)*dtype_size (MLA).

memory_overhead.description: Total usage = model weights + KV cache pool + CUDA graph buffers + activations (official formula from hyperparameter-tuning docs). The static part (weights+KV) is governed by mem_fraction_static; everything else is dynamic headroom. Official guidance: 'reserving 5-8 GB of memory for activations is typically sufficient' on data-center GPUs; if logs show available_gpu_mem of 10-20+ GB, raise mem_fraction_static. Source-code reservation heuristic (MB): 512 fixed metadata + max(chunked_prefill_size, 2048)*1.5 for activations + cuda_graph_max_bs*2 for CUDA graphs + tp_size*pp_size/8*1024 for parallelism buffers. CUDA context itself is ~0.5-1 GB (rough, hardware/driver dependent).
memory_overhead.activation_model: Activation/workspace memory scales with chunked_prefill_size, NOT with full context length (chunked prefill bounds peak prefill activations). Heuristic from source: ~1.5 MB per token of chunk size (rough), i.e. chunk 2048 -> ~3 GB, 4096 -> ~6 GB, 8192 -> ~12 GB reserved. Default chunked_prefill_size auto-tiers by GPU VRAM: <35GB -> 2048, 35-60GB -> 4096, 60-160GB -> 8192, >160GB -> 16384 (fallback 4096). CUDA graph buffers add roughly cuda_graph_max_bs * 2 MB (max_bs auto-tiers ~8-512 by GPU size); raising --cuda-graph-max-bs requires lowering mem_fraction_static. Add ~1 GB per 8 TP*PP ranks for parallelism. OOM-at-prefill fix: reduce chunked-prefill-size to 4096/2048; OOM-at-decode fix: reduce max-running-requests.

savings_techniques.technique: RadixAttention prefix caching (radix tree over KV pool, LRU eviction, cache-aware scheduling)
savings_techniques.saving: Compute/latency: up to 6.4x throughput and up to 3.7x lower latency vs contemporary systems on shared-prefix workloads (paper, 2024 baselines — treat as workload-dependent). Cache hit rates measured in the paper range 50%-99% across benchmarks (few-shot MMLU/HellaSwag at the high end ~90%+, multi-turn chat roughly 75-90% — per-benchmark figures are rough readings of Fig.13); cache-aware scheduling achieves 96% of the optimal hit rate on average. VRAM effect: a prefix shared by N concurrent requests is stored once instead of N times — KV memory for the shared portion scales O(P) not O(N*P) (e.g. 50 concurrent requests sharing a 2,000-token system prompt: ~one copy instead of 50, a 98% reduction on that segment). The cache itself is free: it lives inside the preallocated pool as evictable tokens, so enabling it never increases required VRAM. Hit rate h cuts prefill compute and new-KV writes for the cached fraction proportionally.

Zero internal page fragmentation: with 1 token/page, no slack is ever allocated beyond tokens actually generated. Versus naive contiguous max-length preallocation this eliminates the 60-80% KV waste reported in the vLLM/PagedAttention paper; versus block-paged allocators it also removes the residual per-sequence partial-block waste (avg ~ (block_size-1)/2 tokens/seq, i.e. <4% in vLLM with block 16 — so the incremental gain over vLLM is small, ~1-3%, rough). Tradeoff: some high-perf backends require page>1 (FlashMLA 64, Cutlass MLA 128, FA4 128), reintroducing minor partial-page waste.

50% KV memory vs FP16/BF16 (1 byte vs 2 bytes per element) -> ~2x token capacity in the same pool. Supported on FlashInfer, FA3, Triton, FlashMLA, TRTLLM backends. Mild accuracy risk; e5m2 is scale-free, e4m3 generally more accurate.

75% KV memory vs FP16 (~4x token capacity, plus scale metadata overhead so slightly less in practice — rough). Backend-restricted: FA4, Triton, FlashMLA, Cutlass MLA, TRTLLM MLA; not FlashInfer MHA. Experimental/Blackwell-era; accuracy impact larger than FP8.

savings_techniques.technique: Chunked prefill (default on, size auto-tiered 2048-16384 by GPU VRAM)
savings_techniques.saving: Caps peak prefill activation memory at O(chunk_size) instead of O(prompt_length): a 128K-token prompt prefilled in 8K chunks needs ~1/16 the peak activation workspace of single-shot prefill (rough). Reservation heuristic ~1.5 MB/chunk-token; dropping chunk from 8192 to 2048 frees ~9 GB of required headroom (rough, from source heuristic). Official OOM remedy: reduce to 4096 or 2048.

savings_techniques.technique: Quantized weight loading (AWQ/GPTQ int4, FP8, NVFP4/MXFP4, GGUF, bitsandbytes, compressed-tensors, torchao...)
savings_techniques.saving: Weights at int4 ≈ 75% reduction vs FP16 (plus a few % for scales/zeros — e.g. Llama-3.1-8B ~16 GB -> ~4.5-5.5 GB); FP8 = 50%; NVFP4/MXFP4 ≈ 73-75%. Every GB freed from weights goes directly to the KV pool under mem_fraction_static. Docs recommend offline pre-quantized checkpoints over online --quantization for performance and accuracy; do not combine --quantization with a pre-quantized model.

savings_techniques.technique: HiCache hierarchical KV cache (GPU L1 / host RAM L2 / distributed storage L3: 3FS, Mooncake, NIXL, file)
savings_techniques.saving: Not a VRAM reduction but a VRAM-multiplier for cache capacity: effective prefix cache extends to host RAM and remote storage. Reported: up to 6x throughput and up to 80% TTFT reduction in benchmarks; production: Novita AI hit rate 40%->80%, avg TTFT -56%, QPS 2x; Ant Group: 84% TTFT reduction on cache hit vs full recompute. GPU-assisted transfer kernels give up to 3x CPU-GPU transfer throughput. Host cache size set via --hicache-ratio (multiplier of GPU pool, costs host RAM not VRAM).

For MLA models the pool stores (kv_lora_rank + qk_rope_head_dim) = 576 elements/layer/token (DeepSeek-V3) instead of MHA/GQA 2*kv_heads*head_dim — ~93% smaller than equivalent MHA at 128 heads (model-architecture property, but SGLang allocates the compressed form natively, incl. FP8 MLA pools).

Lowering mem_fraction_static 0.9 -> 0.7 shrinks the KV pool by 0.2*VRAM (16 GB on an 80 GB GPU) to make headroom — the standard OOM fix, at the cost of max concurrency. Disabling/shrinking CUDA graphs (--cuda-graph-max-bs, --disable-cuda-graph) frees roughly cuda_graph_max_bs*2 MB to a few GB (rough). --disable-radix-cache exists but saves no memory (cache is evictable in-pool); it only avoids tree bookkeeping.

frameworks.hardware_support: NVIDIA GPUs (GB200/B300/H100/A100/DGX Spark/RTX 5090), AMD GPUs (MI355/MI300, via AITER/ROCm backends), Intel Xeon CPUs, Google TPUs, Ascend NPUs 'and more' (per official README, June 2026); Apple MLX quant formats (mlx_q4/q8) appear in quantization choices.
frameworks.notes: Key calculator-relevant semantics: (1) mem_fraction_static covers weights+KV ONLY — add 5-8 GB (data-center GPUs) of dynamic overhead on top when computing total footprint; this differs from vLLM's gpu_memory_utilization which is a total cap. (2) Default mem_fraction_static on current main is computed from GPU size via the reserved_mem formula (fallback 0.88); older versions used TP-tiered constants (~0.88 TP1, ~0.81 TP8). (3) KV bytes/token = 2*L*kv_heads*head_dim*dtype_size (MHA/GQA) or L*(kv_lora_rank+qk_rope_head_dim)*dtype_size (MLA); max_total_num_tokens = (mem_fraction_static*VRAM - weights - other static) / bytes_per_token. (4) Per-benchmark radix hit rates (85-95% few-shot, 75-90% multi-turn) circulate in third-party blogs and roughly match paper Fig.13, but the only solid primary quotes are '50% to 99%' range and '96% of optimal on average' — mark per-workload values rough. (5) kv-cache-dtype 'auto' = model dtype, so FP16/BF16 by default; FP8 KV is opt-in (not auto on Hopper, unlike some TRT-LLM presets), except DeepSeek-DSA-on-SM100+ auto-fp8_e4m3 in current main.

corrections.claim: savings_techniques[MLA-native KV pool]: '~93% smaller than equivalent MHA at 128 heads'
corrections.was: ~93% smaller than equivalent MHA at 128 heads
corrections.should_be: ~98% smaller vs true MHA at 128 heads: MLA stores 576 elements/layer/token (kv_lora_rank 512 + qk_rope_head_dim 64, confirmed in DeepSeek-V3 config.json) vs MHA 2*128*128=32,768 (98.2% reduction; 98.6% if using V3's 192-dim K / 128-dim V heads). The widely-cited 93.3% figure is from the DeepSeek-V2 paper and is measured against DeepSeek 67B (a GQA baseline), not 128-head MHA. Either change the number to ~98% or change the baseline attribution.
corrections.source: https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3/raw/main/config.json (kv_lora_rank=512, qk_rope_head_dim=64, num_attention_heads=128) + arithmetic; 93.3% origin: DeepSeek-V2 paper (arXiv:2405.04434) vs DeepSeek 67B

corrections.claim: memory_overhead.activation_model / chunked-prefill savings: 'dropping chunk from 8192 to 2048 frees ~9 GB of required headroom' and the reserved_mem formula as stated
corrections.was: reserved_mem [MB] = 512 + max(chunked_prefill_size,2048)*1.5 + cuda_graph_max_bs*2 + tp_size*pp_size/8*1024 (plus DP-attention extras); dropping chunk 8192->2048 frees ~9 GB
corrections.should_be: Current main adds a floor the researcher missed: `if gpu_mem > 60*1024: reserved_mem = max(reserved_mem, 10*1024)` — on >60 GB GPUs (H100/A100-80/H200/B200) at least 10 GiB is always reserved by the auto default. So dropping chunked_prefill 8192->2048 on an 80 GB GPU only shrinks auto-reserved memory from ~13.4 GiB to the 10 GiB floor (~3.4 GB freed), not ~9 GB; the ~9 GB figure only holds on <60 GB GPUs or with manually-set mem_fraction_static. The formula also omits: +6 GiB reserve for STANDALONE speculative decoding / +4 GiB for EAGLE-style spec decode, +1.5 GiB for piecewise CUDA graphs on MLA backends (+len(prefill_bs)*8 MB non-MLA), and a multimodal/VLM downward adjustment of mem_fraction_static (adjust_mem_fraction_for_vlm).
corrections.source: https://github.com/sgl-project/sglang/blob/main/python/sglang/srt/server_args.py (mem_fraction_static computation block: 'if gpu_mem is not None and gpu_mem > 60 * 1024: reserved_mem = max(reserved_mem, 10 * 1024)'; speculative_algorithm branch adds 4-6 GiB)

corrections.claim: default_kv_dtype / kv-quant technique: FP8 KV 'Supported on FlashInfer, FA3, Triton, FlashMLA, TRTLLM backends' (flat list)
corrections.was: FP8 KV supported on FlashInfer, FA3, Triton, FlashMLA, TRTLLM (no MHA/MLA split)
corrections.should_be: Split by attention type per official support matrix: FP8 KV for MHA = FlashInfer, FA3, Triton, torch_native, TRTLLM MHA, AITER (ROCm), Ascend; FP8 KV for MLA = FlashMLA, Cutlass MLA, TRTLLM MLA, CuteDSL MLA, Ascend MLA — FA3 and FlashInfer do NOT support FP8 KV in MLA mode, and FA4 does not support FP8 KV at all. Matters for DeepSeek-on-Hopper calculator paths (FA3 is the default Hopper backend).

corrections.claim: kv_strategy: 'Default --page-size is 1' (unqualified)
corrections.was: default page size 1 universally
corrections.should_be: Default is 1 on CUDA (and most platforms), but current main defaults to 64 on MUSA devices and on ROCm when SGLANG_AITER_KV_CACHE_LAYOUT=vectorized_5d, and forces 16 for Qwen3-VL with the aiter unified-attention backend. Negligible for NVIDIA-focused calculators; worth a footnote for AMD.

corrections.claim: FP4 KV backend list: 'FA4, Triton, FlashMLA, Cutlass MLA, TRTLLM MLA; not FlashInfer MHA'
corrections.was: FP4 list omits several supported backends
corrections.should_be: Correct as far as it goes ('not FlashInfer MHA' verified), but incomplete: FP4 KV is also supported on FlashInfer MLA, torch_native, flex_attention, and TRTLLM MHA (code: KV4_ATTENTION_MLA_BACKEND_CHOICES=[cutlass_mla, flashinfer, trtllm_mla, flashmla]; KV4_ATTENTION_MHA_BACKEND_CHOICES=[triton, torch_native, flex_attention, trtllm_mha]). fp4_e2m1 is mxfp4-only, requires CUDA 12.8+ and PyTorch 2.8+.

missing: Hybrid SWA KV pool for sliding-window models (Gemma 3, GPT-OSS, etc.): SGLang allocates a separate, smaller KV pool for sliding-window layers (server_args: disable_hybrid_swa_memory, swa_full_tokens_ratio default 0.8), so KV bytes/token must be computed per layer type (full-attention layers at full context, SWA layers capped near window size). The researcher's single bytes_per_token = 2*L*kv_heads*head_dim formula overestimates KV for these models in SGLang — directly calculator-relevant.

Mamba/hybrid-SSM state cache for hybrid models (Qwen3-Next, Falcon-H1, NemotronH style): separate per-request SSM state pool outside the token KV pool, controlled by max_mamba_cache_size, mamba_ssm_dtype, mamba_full_memory_ratio (default 0.9), MambaRadixCache requires page_size=1. This is additional VRAM not captured by the KV bytes/token formula.
--cpu-offload-gb: weight offloading to CPU RAM (default 0) — a VRAM-reduction technique entirely absent from savings_techniques.

Speculative decoding memory: draft model weights + extra CUDA graphs; current main's auto mem_fraction reserves +6 GiB (STANDALONE) or +4 GiB (EAGLE-family) — affects default KV pool size when spec decode is on.

10 GiB minimum reserved_mem on GPUs >60 GB in the auto mem_fraction_static default (also listed as a correction) — a calculator using the researcher's formula will overestimate the default KV pool on H100/H200/B200 when chunked_prefill_size is small.

Multimodal/VLM adjustment: adjust_mem_fraction_for_vlm further lowers mem_fraction_static for vision-language models (image-processing memory), so VLM KV capacity is lower than the text-only formula predicts.
Quantization choices present in current main but absent from the list: modelopt_mixed, modelslim (Ascend NPU), unquant (trivial).

Backend page sizes not listed: TRTLLM MLA and CuteDSL MLA require page_size in {32, 64} (researcher only gave TRTLLM MHA); FA4 MLA uses page_size=1 (the 128 requirement is non-MLA only); FlashInfer MLA requires page_size=1.
verification.summary: Re-verified against sglang main (server_args.py, model_runner_kv_cache_mixin.py fetched 2026-06-12), official docs (attention_backend, hyperparameter_tuning), arXiv:2312.07104 HTML, the HiCache blog, the README, and DeepSeek-V3's HF config.json. Spot-checked >80% of numeric claims; the research is unusually accurate. CONFIRMED: default page_size=1 (CUDA); forced page sizes FlashMLA=64, Cutlass MLA=128, FA4-MHA=128, TRTLLM-MHA {16,32,64}; kv-cache-dtype choices {auto, fp8_e5m2, fp8_e4m3, bf16/bfloat16, fp4_e2m1}; auto=model dtype with DeepSeek-DSA-on-SM>=10 auto-fp8_e4m3 (SM<10 -> bfloat16); mem_fraction_static = (weights+KV)/GPU capacity exactly as code-commented, default = (gpu_mem - reserved_mem)/gpu_mem with reserved_mem = 512 + max(chunked_prefill,2048)*1.5 + cuda_graph_max_bs*2 + tp*pp/8*1024 MB (+DP extras), fallback 0.88; chunked-prefill tiers 2048/<35GB, 4096/35-60GB, 8192/60-160GB, 16384/>160GB (fallback 4096); cuda_graph max_bs tiers 8-512 (fallback 160); MLA pool dim = kv_lora_rank+qk_rope_head_dim = 576 for DeepSeek-V3 (512+64 per HF config); paper figures 6.4x throughput, 3.7x latency, 50-99% hit rate, 96%-of-optimal all quoted verbatim in the paper; docs' 5-8 GB activation guidance, OOM remedies, and 10-20 GB available_gpu_mem guidance verbatim; HiCache numbers (6x, 80% TTFT, Novita 40->80%/-56%/2x, Ant 84%, 3x transfer, --hicache-ratio, 3FS/Mooncake/NIXL/file) verbatim; README hardware list verbatim; full quantization choices list matches main. CORRECTIONS (2 substantive): (1) MLA '~93% smaller than MHA-128' should be ~98% (93.3% is DeepSeek-V2-paper-vs-DeepSeek-67B-GQA); (2) the auto reserved-mem formula has a 10 GiB floor on >60 GB GPUs that caps the chunk-size savings claim (~3.4 GB freed, not ~9 GB, on H100-class with auto defaults) plus omitted spec-decode/VLM reserves. Minor: FP8-KV backend list needs an MHA/MLA split (FA3 lacks MLA-FP8), FP4-KV list incomplete, page_size=1 default has MUSA/ROCm-5D exceptions. MISSED (calculator-relevant): hybrid SWA KV pool for sliding-window models (per-layer KV sizing), Mamba/SSM state cache for hybrid models, --cpu-offload-gb, spec-decode and VLM memory reserves.

frameworks.name: llama.cpp
frameworks.kv_strategy: Contiguous static allocation: the full KV cache for the configured context (-c/--ctx-size) is allocated up front at model load — no paged attention. Per-layer KV bytes = n_ctx * n_head_kv * head_dim * 2 (K+V) * bytes_per_elem * n_layer (GQA-aware: uses n_head_kv, not n_head). Since PR #13194 the unified KV cache (llama_kv_cache_unified) can cache a subset of layers, enabling interleaved sliding-window (iSWA) caches for models like Gemma 3 where SWA layers allocate only window-sized storage (~1/5 of full-context KV for Gemma 3's 5:1 interleave with 1024-token window; disable via --swa-full). For llama-server with --parallel N: legacy behavior splits ctx_size/N statically per slot; with --kv-unified (default when slot count is auto in current builds) all slots dynamically share one KV buffer instead of fixed partitions. CAUTION for a calculator: current builds (Debian unstable manpage) default --ctx-size to 0 = load the model's FULL training context (e.g. 131072 for Llama 3.1), so default KV allocation can be enormous; older 2024-2025 builds defaulted to 4096.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: f16 for both K and V (--cache-type-k / --cache-type-v default f16). Options exist per-cache-half. Quantized V cache requires flash attention; Flash Attention default changed to 'auto'/on in Aug 2025 builds (turn off with -fa off), so KV quant is now usable out of the box on supported backends.
frameworks.hardware_support: CPU (x86/ARM), NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm/HIP, Apple Metal (unified memory), Vulkan, SYCL (Intel), OpenCL, Moore Threads MUSA; partial GPU offload via -ngl on all GPU backends

memory_overhead.description: VRAM = weights(offloaded layers) + KV cache + GPU compute buffer + backend fixed overhead. CUDA context/runtime costs ~300-800 MB per GPU per process before any tensor is allocated (commonly ~0.3-0.5 GB; ~0.75 GB observed on high-end cards); Metal has essentially no equivalent context overhead but caps GPU-usable memory at recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize ≈ 75% of unified RAM (e.g. ~96 GB on a 128 GB M1 Ultra; raisable via sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb). Compute buffers are markedly smaller on Metal than CUDA for the same model (e.g. ~266 MB Metal vs substantially larger on CUDA per discussion #9784). Host-side: weights are mmap'd by default (--no-mmap to disable), so CPU RAM for offloaded weights is page-cache, not resident allocation.
memory_overhead.activation_model: Compute (graph) buffer scales ~linearly with n_ctx and with batch size; no closed-form formula — maintainers recommend measuring at 2-3 ctx sizes and fitting Memory = k*n_ctx + o per buffer (discussion #10068). Reference points: Gemma 2 9B, n_ctx=3072/n_batch=128 -> 126.75 MiB CUDA compute buffer (+1008 MiB KV, +1.95 MiB output, +4.75 MiB host compute); n_ctx=4096/n_batch=2048 -> 507 MiB. Without FA the buffer holds the f32 attention-score matrix (n_kv * n_ubatch * n_head) so it grows steeply with context; with FA enabled it is much flatter — model FA-on as a few hundred MiB plus a small linear term, and recalibrate per backend (CUDA vs Metal differ). Defaults: --batch-size 2048, --ubatch-size 512 (compute buffer scales with ubatch).

frameworks.preallocates: Yes for KV: full KV cache for the configured context is allocated at load time regardless of actual prompt length, so 'required VRAM' = weights + full-context KV + compute buffer at the configured n_ctx. Does NOT grab a fraction of total VRAM like vLLM's gpu_memory_utilization — it allocates exactly what the config implies. With current defaults (ctx-size 0 = model max, -ngl auto), it auto-fits layers to free VRAM.
quant_support: GGUF only: Q4_0/Q4_1/Q5_0/Q5_1/Q8_0 (legacy, 32-weight blocks + fp16 scale: 4.5/5.0/5.5/6.0/8.5 bpw)
K-quants (256-weight super-blocks, block-level bpw incl. scale overhead: Q2_K 2.5625, Q3_K 3.4375, Q4_K 4.5, Q5_K 5.5, Q6_K 6.5625)

measured MODEL-level bpw for Llama-3.1-8B incl. mixes & embed/output tensors (use these for file-size estimates): IQ1_S 2.00, IQ2_XXS 2.38, IQ2_M 2.93, Q2_K 3.16, IQ3_XXS 3.25, Q3_K_S 3.64, Q3_K_M ~3.9, Q3_K_L 4.30, IQ4_XS ~4.3, Q4_K_S 4.67, Q4_K_M 4.89, Q5_K_S 5.57, Q5_K_M 5.70, Q6_K 6.56, Q8_0 8.50, F16 16.0
_M/_L mixes use Q6_K for half of attn.wv and ffn.w2 tensors plus 6-bit output.weight, which is why model-level bpw exceeds block-level bpw

savings_techniques.saving: q8_0 ≈ 47% KV reduction vs f16 (8.5 vs 16 bpw; e.g. 768 MiB -> 408 MiB for a 128K-ctx example); negligible quality loss (+0.002-0.05 perplexity reported). V-cache quant requires flash attention; without FA, quantized cache must be dequantized per step (up to ~37% tg slowdown observed for q4_0 at 110K ctx).
q4_0 ≈ 72% KV reduction vs f16 (4.5 vs 16 bpw; 768 MiB -> 216 MiB in same example); noticeable quality loss possible, worse on high-GQA models.

savings_techniques.technique: Flash attention (-fa, default auto/on since Aug 2025)
savings_techniques.saving: Removes the f32 attention-score matrix from the compute buffer, making compute-buffer growth with context near-flat instead of linear-steep; also prerequisite for V-cache quantization. Rough: saves hundreds of MiB to multiple GiB at 32K+ contexts.

savings_techniques.technique: iSWA KV cache for sliding-window models (Gemma 2/3 etc.)
savings_techniques.saving: SWA layers store only window-sized KV (Gemma 3: 1024-token window, 5:1 SWA:full ratio) -> ~80% (≈1/5) KV memory vs full-context cache at long contexts; on by default, --swa-full reverts to uncompressed (and re-enables cache shifting).

savings_techniques.technique: Unified KV buffer across server slots (--kv-unified)
savings_techniques.saving: Parallel slots share one ctx_size budget dynamically instead of static ctx/N partitions — avoids N-fold over-allocation when slots are idle; default when slot count is auto.

savings_techniques.technique: Partial GPU offload (-ngl N, default auto)
savings_techniques.saving: Only N layers' weights AND their KV slices go to VRAM; rest stays in (mmap'd) host RAM. VRAM ≈ (N/n_layer) * (weights+KV) + compute buffer + backend overhead. Linear, user-controllable trade against speed.

Q4_K_M ≈ 4.89 bpw effective -> ~69% smaller than F16 (4.58 GiB vs 14.96 GiB for Llama-3.1-8B); Q8_0 8.5 bpw -> ~47% smaller; IQ2_M 2.93 bpw -> ~82% smaller (quality cost). Block scale overhead already included in these figures.
Saves host RAM (weights are page-cache, can exceed physical RAM), not VRAM; --no-mmap forces full resident copy and fails if model > RAM.

Compute buffer scales with ubatch (rough: Gemma2-9B 507 MiB at n_batch 2048 vs 126.75 MiB at 128); reducing ubatch 512->128 typically saves a few hundred MiB at long contexts at the cost of prefill speed.

frameworks.notes: No paged attention, no vLLM-style preemption: OOM risk is at load time, which makes calculator math deterministic. KV formula: bytes = n_layer * n_ctx * n_head_kv * head_dim * 2 * bpw/8 (x2 caches K and V already included via the *2). Example check: Gemma 2 9B at n_ctx 3072 f16 -> 1008 MiB matches discussion #10068. Asymmetric K/V quant combos may not offload to GPU (issue #20866). Metal vs CUDA: Metal unified memory means 'VRAM' = wired system RAM capped at ~75% (iogpu.wired_limit_mb to raise); CUDA pays 300-800 MB context per GPU per process and larger compute buffers.

frameworks.name: Ollama
frameworks.kv_strategy: Wraps llama.cpp/GGML (plus its own 'new engine' runners) — same contiguous, load-time KV allocation, no paged attention. KEY DIVERGENCE: total KV = OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL * num_ctx, statically multiplied per slot (a 2K ctx with 4 parallel slots allocates an 8K-token cache); it does not use llama.cpp's newer shared/unified slot budget. OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL now defaults to 1 (2024-2025 versions defaulted to auto = 4 when memory allowed — version-dependent for the calculator). Since v0.12 (Sept 2025, 'new model scheduling') Ollama measures exact memory in estimate/allocate/commit phases instead of heuristic estimation, auto-computes GPU layer split (weights + KV + compute-graph per layer), and nvidia-smi now matches ollama ps. Each loaded model runs in its own runner subprocess (own CUDA context); OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS default = 3 * GPU count.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: f16. Changed globally (not per-model) via OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0|q4_0, and it ONLY takes effect when flash attention is active: historically required OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1 (off by default); since v0.11.8+ FA is enabled by default for selected new-engine models (gpt-oss 20B/120B, later qwen3vl etc.) on supporting hardware, silently falling back to standard attention (and f16 KV) otherwise.
kv_dtype_options: q8_0 (~1/2 the KV memory of f16)
q4_0 (~1/4 the KV memory of f16)
frameworks.hardware_support: NVIDIA CUDA (compute 5.0+), AMD ROCm, Apple Metal, CPU fallback; Vulkan experimental in recent builds. FA-by-default models gated to newer GPUs (Ampere+ NVIDIA, RDNA3+ AMD per community testing).

memory_overhead.description: Same component model as llama.cpp (weights + KV + compute buffer + CUDA context ~300-800 MB per GPU per runner process), plus Ollama-specific costs: each concurrently loaded model is a separate runner process paying its own CUDA context; the scheduler holds models resident for keep_alive (default 5 min). Since v0.12 the allocator reserves measured-exact buffers up front, reducing OOM crashes and over-allocation vs the old estimate-based scheduler. mmap divergence: Ollama passes --no-mmap to its runner in some configurations (fully-offloaded GPU loads), so host RAM behavior differs from stock llama.cpp; use_mmap is settable per-Modelfile.
memory_overhead.activation_model: Identical to llama.cpp underneath: compute/graph buffer grows ~linearly with num_ctx and batch (Ollama fixes n_batch=512), flatter with FA on. For estimation reuse the llama.cpp linear-fit approach; Ollama's v0.12+ scheduler itself accounts 'model weights, KV cache, and compute graph overhead' per layer when deciding GPU offload.

frameworks.preallocates: Yes — full KV for num_parallel * num_ctx is allocated at model load (measured-exact since v0.12). It does NOT reserve a fixed fraction of VRAM; if the total doesn't fit, Ollama automatically offloads layers to CPU rather than failing, so 'required VRAM' is soft: the model still runs, just slower. ollama ps shows the actual GPU/CPU split and context in effect.
quant_support: GGUF formats identical to llama.cpp (K-quants, I-quants, legacy Q4_0/Q8_0, F16/BF16) — same effective bpw table applies
Library models typically ship Q4_K_M by default (~4.89 bpw effective)

savings_techniques.saving: ~50% of KV memory vs f16 with negligible quality loss (+0.002-0.05 perplexity on Qwen2.5-Coder benchmark); requires flash attention active or it silently does nothing. Official FAQ: 'approximately 1/2 the memory of f16'.
~75% KV memory reduction vs f16 ('approximately 1/4 the memory of f16'); quality impact larger on high-GQA models (e.g. Qwen2) per official FAQ.

savings_techniques.technique: Flash attention (OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1; default-on for select models since v0.11.8)
savings_techniques.saving: 'Significantly reduce memory usage as the context size grows' (compute-buffer flattening, same mechanism as llama.cpp); prerequisite for KV quantization.

KV scales linearly with num_ctx; rough 0.2-0.4 GB saved going 8K->4K on a 7-8B f16-KV model. DIVERGENCE from llama.cpp: server default is 4096 tokens (was 2048 until v0.5.13, Feb 2025); the desktop app now auto-tiers by VRAM: 4K (<24 GB), 32K (24-48 GB), 256K (>=48 GB) — a calculator must branch on this.

Auto-fits as many layers as free VRAM allows (weights + per-layer KV + graph), spilling the rest to CPU RAM — converts hard OOM into a throughput penalty; exact since v0.12 scheduler (vendor-claimed large reduction in OOM crashes, maximized GPU utilization).

savings_techniques.technique: iSWA KV cache for Gemma-family (new engine)
savings_techniques.saving: Ollama's new engine implemented interleaved sliding-window KV for Gemma 3 before llama.cpp did — ~1/5 the KV memory of full-context cache at long contexts (5:1 SWA ratio, 1024 window).

savings_techniques.technique: Weight quantization via GGUF library tags
savings_techniques.saving: Same as llama.cpp: Q4_K_M ~69% smaller than F16, Q8_0 ~47% smaller (scale overhead included: 4.89 / 8.5 bpw effective).
KV memory scales by num_parallel * num_ctx; dropping legacy auto-4 to 1 cuts KV allocation 4x for single-user use. Default is already 1 in current versions.

frameworks.notes: Divergence summary vs stock llama.cpp for a calculator: (1) context default 4096 via env/server vs llama.cpp's current 'model max' default — and app-tiered 4K/32K/256K by VRAM; (2) KV quant is a global env var, not per-model flags, and gated on FA; (3) parallel slots statically multiply KV (no --kv-unified sharing); (4) auto CPU spill means VRAM requirement is advisory, not fatal; (5) per-model runner subprocess each pays CUDA context; (6) Ollama sometimes disables mmap where llama.cpp defaults it on; (7) since v0.12 memory is measured exactly (three-phase) rather than estimated, so older calculator fudge factors (~+10-20%) should be reduced for v0.12+. Historic default num_ctx 2048 (pre-Feb-2025) still appears in older guides — version matters.

corrections.claim: Ollama default context window history: 'server default is 4096 tokens (was 2048 until v0.5.13, Feb 2025)'
corrections.was: default changed from 2048 to 4096 in v0.5.13 (Feb 2025)
corrections.should_be: v0.5.13 (Feb 27, 2025) only ADDED the OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH env var with default still 2048 (release notes mention only the env var). The default flipped to 4096 in commit 44b466e 'config: update default context length to 4096' (envconfig/config.go, Uint("OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH", 2048) -> 4096), authored 2025-04-29, first shipped in v0.6.7 (GitHub compare API: commit absent from v0.6.6, contained in v0.6.7).

corrections.claim: Ollama VRAM-tiered context defaults attributed to 'the desktop app now auto-tiers by VRAM: 4K (<24 GB), 32K (24-48 GB), 256K (>=48 GB)'
corrections.was: tiering described as a desktop-app behavior, with flat 4096 as the server default in current versions
corrections.should_be: The VRAM tiers (<24 GiB -> 4,096; 24-48 GiB -> 32,768; >=48 GiB -> 262,144) became Ollama's general default context lengths in v0.15.5 (late 2025), not a desktop-app-only feature — docs.ollama.com/context-length documents the tiers as the defaults, and GitHub issues #14073 ('New default context lengths will break', filed against the server behavior) and #14116 ('Tiered context length can exhaust VRAM') confirm server-side scope. Flat 4096 (still stated in the FAQ) is stale for v0.15.5+; current builds are v0.30.x (June 2026). A calculator should branch on version: <v0.6.7 = 2048, v0.6.7–v0.15.4 = 4096, v0.15.5+ = VRAM-tiered.
corrections.confidence: high on version/values, medium on app-vs-server scoping detail

corrections.claim: Apple Metal GPU-usable memory cap 'recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize ≈ 75% of unified RAM'
corrections.was: blanket ~75% of unified RAM
corrections.should_be: ~75% only on high-RAM Apple Silicon (64 GB+ per multiple measurements; some sources put the kernel threshold at >36 GB); Macs below that get ~2/3 (~66-67%) of RAM. E.g. 16 GB Mac -> ~10.7 GB usable, 32 GB -> ~21.3 GB, 64 GB -> 48 GB, 128 GB -> 96 GB. The researcher's 128 GB/96 GB example is correct, but applying 75% to the most common 8-32 GB Macs overstates usable memory by ~8 points; a calculator must use the two-tier fraction. iogpu.wired_limit_mb override claim is correct.

corrections.claim: Model-level bpw for Llama-3.1-8B: 'IQ4_XS ~4.3'
corrections.was: IQ4_XS ~4.3 bpw model-level
corrections.should_be: IQ4_XS = 4.4597 bpw / 4.17 GiB per the official quantize README table (block-level IQ4_XS is 4.25 bpw, which is likely what got conflated). All other table values verified exact: IQ1_S 2.0042, IQ2_XXS 2.3824, IQ2_M 2.9294, Q2_K 3.1593, IQ3_XXS 3.2548, Q3_K_S 3.6429, Q3_K_M 3.9960, Q3_K_L 4.2979, Q4_K_S 4.6672, Q4_K_M 4.8944/4.58 GiB, Q5_K_S 5.5704, Q5_K_M 5.7036, Q6_K 6.5633, Q8_0 8.5008/7.95 GiB, F16 16.0005/14.96 GiB.

corrections.claim: Ollama flash-attention-by-default model gating: 'gated to newer GPUs (Ampere+ NVIDIA, RDNA3+ AMD per community testing)' and FA-default list 'gpt-oss 20B/120B, later qwen3vl etc.'
corrections.was: hedged as community testing; FA-default list implied small/uncertain
corrections.should_be: Now officially documented, and the list is larger: v0.11.8 (Aug 2025) enabled FA by default for gpt-oss 20B/120B; v0.12.4 added Qwen 3 / Qwen 3 Coder; by v0.30.0 (May 2026) FA is on by default for qwen3, qwen3moe, qwen3vl, qwen3vlmoe, gemma3, gpt-oss, mistral3 on Ampere-or-newer NVIDIA and RDNA3-or-newer AMD. So for current versions, KV-cache quantization works out-of-the-box for these families without OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1.
corrections.confidence: high for v0.11.8/v0.12.4, medium for exact v0.30.0 model list (secondary source)

corrections.claim: Ollama v0.12 'measures exact memory in estimate/allocate/commit phases'
corrections.was: three named phases (estimate/allocate/commit) attributed to the new scheduler
corrections.should_be: The official blog (Sept 23, 2025) confirms exact measurement ('measure the exact amount of memory required compared to an estimation'), nvidia-smi matching ollama ps, and fewer OOMs — but mentions NO three-phase estimate/allocate/commit terminology; that detail comes only from the third-party glukhov.org blog. Keep the exact-measurement claim, drop or soften the phase names.
corrections.confidence: high that the phases are absent from the primary source (they may exist in code, but are unverified)

missing: MoE CPU expert-offload (llama.cpp --cpu-moe / --n-cpu-moe N, and --override-tensor regex, added Aug 2025): keeps expert FFN tensors in host RAM while attention/KV stay on GPU — the single biggest VRAM-saving technique for MoE models (runs gpt-oss-120B / Qwen3-MoE / GLM-4.x on 8-24 GB GPUs). A calculator that models MoE VRAM without this will be badly wrong for the most popular 2025-2026 local models. Ollama exposes no equivalent flag.

MLA models (DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2): llama.cpp uses an MLA KV cache (PR #12801) where per-token-per-layer KV = (kv_lora_rank + qk_rope_head_dim) elements (e.g. 512+64 for DeepSeek V3), NOT n_kv_heads*head_dim*2 — the researcher's universal KV formula does not apply to these architectures and would massively mis-estimate.

Recurrent/hybrid-state models (Mamba2, Jamba, Granite 4, LFM2, Qwen3-Next): fixed-size recurrent state per sequence instead of context-linear KV; both llama.cpp and Ollama run several of these — the linear n_ctx KV formula does not apply.
--no-kv-offload (-nkvo): llama.cpp flag keeping the entire KV cache in host RAM while weights stay in VRAM — changes the VRAM/RAM split formula and is a common fit-it-anyway technique.

MXFP4 quantization type (native format of gpt-oss GGUFs, ~4.25 bpw block-level) absent from the GGUF quant list; also ternary TQ1_0/TQ2_0 (BitNet). Relevant because gpt-oss is among the most-run Ollama models and ships only in MXFP4.

Multimodal projector / vision tower (mmproj) VRAM: Gemma 3, Qwen3-VL, llama3.2-vision etc. load an extra ~0.5-2 GB of vision weights (plus image-embedding compute buffers) on top of the text model; Ollama library vision models include this by default — not accounted for anywhere in the research.

Multi-GPU splitting (llama.cpp --tensor-split, -sm layer|row): determines per-GPU VRAM requirement, and the ~300-800 MB CUDA context cost is paid on EACH GPU; Ollama OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD similarly. A multi-GPU can-I-run-it verdict needs this.
OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD environment variable (reserves a fixed VRAM headroom per GPU) — directly shifts the calculator's usable-VRAM number.
Speculative decoding (llama-server --model-draft) adds the full draft model weights + its KV to VRAM — a sizing input if the calculator supports it.

Version currency: Ollama is at v0.30.x as of June 2026 (and a Gemma 4 era began ~v0.21.0, April 2026); the research's 'current' framing is v0.12-era (Sept 2025). The defaults that changed since: VRAM-tiered context (v0.15.5) and the expanded FA-by-default model list (v0.30.0).
verification.summary: Re-verified ~25 numeric/default claims against primary sources. CONFIRMED: all current llama-server defaults via the Debian unstable manpage (--ctx-size default 0 = loaded from model, -ngl auto, -fa auto, K/V cache type f16 with allowed set f32/f16/bf16/q8_0/q4_0/q4_1/iq4_nl/q5_0/q5_1, -b 2048/-ub 512, -np -1=auto, --kv-unified 'enabled if number of slots is auto', --swa-full false, mmap on); PR #13194 (merged May 20, 2025, iSWA dual-cache, SWA cache sized PAD(n_swa*n_seq_max+n_batch)); discussion #10068 figures (1008 MiB KV / 126.75 MiB CUDA compute / 1.95 MiB output / 4.75 MiB host for Gemma2-9B at ctx 3072, plus the linear k*n_ctx+o fitting advice) — and I independently re-derived the KV number from architecture params (42 layers x 3072 ctx x 8 kv-heads x 256 head-dim x 2 (K+V) x 2 B = exactly 1008 MiB), validating the stated KV formula; discussion #20969 (768->408->216 MiB for f16/q8_0/q4_0 = -47%/-72%, 37% tg slowdown for q4_0 at 110K without on-quant compute, FA mandatory for V-quant); discussion #9784 (Metal 266.50 MiB compute buffer, CUDA runtime overhead); issue #20866 exists with the stated asymmetric-K/V-offload bug; quantize README bpw table matches to 2 decimals (one exception); legacy/K-quant block bpw values all arithmetically correct; FA-auto default landed late Aug 2025; Ollama FAQ confirms q8_0 ~1/2 and q4_0 ~1/4 of f16 KV, OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL default 1, OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS = 3*GPUs, keep_alive 5 min; v0.11.8 FA-default for gpt-oss; Sept 23, 2025 scheduling blog (exact measurement, nvidia-smi==ollama ps, fewer OOMs); Ollama mmap divergence corroborated (PR #6854, issue #10104). CORRECTIONS (6, mostly version/scope): the 2048->4096 Ollama default landed in v0.6.7 (commit 44b466e, Apr 29, 2025), not v0.5.13; VRAM-tiered context defaults are general v0.15.5+ behavior, not desktop-app-only; Metal cap is two-tier (~67% below 64 GB, 75% above) not flat 75% — material for common 8-32 GB Macs; IQ4_XS is 4.46 not ~4.3 bpw; Ollama FA-default model list is now official and much larger (v0.30.0: qwen3/qwen3moe/qwen3vl/qwen3vlmoe/gemma3/gpt-oss/mistral3 on Ampere+/RDNA3+); the 'estimate/allocate/commit' phase naming is not in the primary blog. BIGGEST GAPS: no MoE CPU expert-offload (--cpu-moe), no MLA KV formula for DeepSeek-family, no recurrent-state models, no mmproj/vision VRAM, no MXFP4 — the first two will produce order-of-magnitude-wrong verdicts for gpt-oss-120B and DeepSeek-R1 class models if the generic formulas are applied. Overall the research is solid on llama.cpp mechanics and arithmetic; weaknesses are Ollama version timeline precision and architecture-specific KV exceptions.

frameworks.name: TensorRT-LLM
frameworks.kv_strategy: Paged KV cache (default kv_cache_type=paged; 'continuous' and 'disabled' also exist). Blocks of tokens_per_block=32 by default (trtllm-build default; configurable 8/16/32/64/128 — NVIDIA's KV-reuse blog recommends tuning block size, worth up to ~7% TTFT on Llama-70B/H100). Blocks live in a preallocated GPU pool sized at startup; reusable blocks from prior requests are indexed in a radix search tree for prefix/KV reuse, with priority-based LRU eviction (priorities 0-100) and optional offload of evicted blocks to pinned host memory (host_cache_size, default 0 = disabled). Per-layer variable attention windows (max_attention_window) bound KV growth for sliding-window models.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: KvCacheConfig.dtype default is 'auto' = inherit from the checkpoint's quantization config (runtime log: "KV cache quantization set to 'auto'. Using checkpoint KV quantization."). For unquantized checkpoints this means KV follows model dtype (fp16/bf16). For ModelOpt-prequantized FP8 checkpoints whose hf_quant_config sets kv_cache_quant_algo=FP8 (most nvidia/*-FP8 HF checkpoints), you get FP8 KV cache by default on Hopper/Blackwell/Ada. There is NO unconditional fp8-KV-on-Hopper auto-upgrade for plain fp16 checkpoints — it tracks the checkpoint.
kv_dtype_options: auto (default — from checkpoint quant config, else model dtype)
fp8 (E4M3; storage supported Ampere+, FP8 attention/FMHA compute on Ada/Hopper/Blackwell; ~50% KV memory vs fp16)
int8 (legacy TRT flow, kv_cache_quant_mode; ~50% vs fp16)
nvfp4 KV cache (Blackwell sm100/103 only, requires offline ModelOpt quantization; 50% vs fp8, ~75% vs fp16)

memory_overhead.description: Total = weights + activation/workspace + I/O & runtime buffers + decoder buffers + CUDA context (+ NCCL for multi-GPU) + KV pool (grabs 90% of whatever is left, by default). Official memory-doc example log for a large engine: 'Allocated 1134.01 MiB for execution context memory' (TensorRT activation workspace), 'Allocated 43.29 MB GPU memory for runtime buffers', 'Allocated 180.30 MB GPU memory for decoder', then 'Allocated 63.44 GiB for max tokens in paged KV cache (259840)'. In the TensorRT engine flow, activation memory is fixed at build time from max shapes and reused across layers; in the (now-default) PyTorch flow, activations go through the torch allocator and CUDA graph capture adds extra memory per captured batch size (rough: hundreds of MB).
memory_overhead.activation_model: Activation ≈ max_num_tokens × constant-per-model (official doc: 'activation size will be close to max number of input tokens * some constant factor'); reducing max_num_tokens reduces it almost linearly, and TensorRT reuses memory between layers so only ~1 layer-block's worth is live. Defaults: max_num_tokens=8192, max_batch_size=2048. With chunked prefill enabled, the activation buffer is sized by the chunk size instead of the longest prompt, decoupling activation memory from context length. Rough calculator rule: ~0.5-1.5 GB for max_num_tokens=8192 on 7-70B class models (the 1.13 GiB doc example), scaling linearly with max_num_tokens; baseline_gb above (~1.5 GB, rough) covers CUDA context (~0.3-0.8 GB, driver/GPU dependent, undocumented) + runtime buffers (~43 MB) + decoder (~180 MB) + misc, excluding the scaling activation term.

frameworks.preallocates: YES — paged KV pool is preallocated at startup: kv_cache_free_gpu_mem_fraction / KvCacheConfig.free_gpu_memory_fraction, default 0.90, is the fraction of FREE GPU memory remaining AFTER weights+activations are loaded (docs recommend up to 0.95 if the GPU is dedicated). Capped by max_tokens if both set (min wins). CRITICAL semantic difference from vLLM for a calculator: vLLM's gpu_memory_utilization=0.9 is a fraction of TOTAL VRAM (weights included, hard budget); TRT-LLM's 0.9 is of leftover free memory, so the process organically consumes ~weights + activations + 90% of the rest — i.e., nearly the whole GPU regardless of model size, and 'required VRAM' = weights + activation + overhead, with KV capacity being whatever remains.
quant_support: FP8 (per-tensor, block-scaling, rowwise) — Ada/Hopper/Blackwell; ~2x weight reduction vs FP16
NVFP4 — Blackwell only (sm100/103/120); 3.5x weight reduction vs FP16, 1.8x vs FP8 (4.5 bits/value incl. FP8 scale per 16-block)
INT4 AWQ (W4A16) — Ampere+; W4A8 AWQ on Ada/Hopper/Blackwell; ~3.5-4x weight reduction
INT8 SmoothQuant (W8A8) — legacy TRT flow; ~2x weight reduction
FP8 KV cache (Ampere+ storage), INT8 KV cache, NVFP4 KV cache (Blackwell)
NOT supported: GGUF, EXL2 (use llama.cpp/exllama for those)

savings_techniques.saving: Near-zero KV fragmentation vs naive contiguous per-request allocation (same approach as vLLM PagedAttention: naive allocation wastes ~60-80% of KV memory, paging cuts waste to <4%; TRT-LLM publishes no own figure — vLLM paper number used as proxy, rough). Pool-based: blocks allocated on demand from preallocated pool, tokens_per_block=32 default.

savings_techniques.technique: KV cache reuse / prefix caching (radix tree, enable_block_reuse)
savings_techniques.saving: Up to 5x faster TTFT for shared-system-prompt workloads (NVIDIA blog, early KV reuse); priority-based eviction raises cache hit rate ~20% (workload-dependent); KV block size tuning gives up to 7% TTFT on Llama-70B/H100. Saves recompute, not steady-state capacity; partial (per-block) reuse enabled by default.

50% KV memory vs FP16/BF16 (8 vs 16 bits + negligible per-tensor scales) → ~2x max concurrent tokens. Storage works on Ampere+; FP8 attention compute (use_fp8_context_fmha) needs Ada/Hopper/Blackwell. Default-on only when the checkpoint's quant config requests it (auto mode).

50% KV memory vs FP8 (≈75% vs FP16) → doubles context length/batch size vs FP8 KV; up to 3x TTFT at long context, ~20% higher cache hit rate vs FP8; <1% accuracy loss on MMLU-PRO/LiveCodeBench/Ruler-64K. Requires offline ModelOpt quantization.
50% KV memory vs FP16 (exact arithmetic).

Decouples activation memory from prompt length: max_num_tokens can stay small (e.g. 8192 default) instead of >= longest prompt; activation buffer sized by chunk, freed memory goes to KV pool. Saving ≈ (max_ISL − chunk_size) × per-token activation constant — for 128k-context configs this is multiple GB (rough). NVIDIA recommends always enabling it; worst case ~neutral perf.

savings_techniques.technique: Weight quantization (FP8 / NVFP4 / INT4 AWQ / GPTQ / INT8 SmoothQuant)
savings_techniques.saving: FP8: 2.0x weight memory vs FP16 (1 B/param). NVFP4: 3.5x vs FP16, 1.8x vs FP8 (4.5 bits/param incl. scales; Blackwell only). INT4 AWQ/GPTQ W4A16: ~3.5-4x vs FP16 (~4.25-4.5 bits/param with group scales, rough). INT8 SmoothQuant W8A8: 2x. Exact for the weight term only; KV/activation unchanged unless KV quant also enabled.
Moves evicted/reusable KV blocks to pinned host RAM instead of recompute — saves GPU KV capacity for hot blocks at PCIe-transfer cost; default 0 (off). Blocks below priority 35 skip offload.
KV per windowed layer bounded at window_size tokens instead of full seq — e.g. Gemma-style 1k-window layers at 64k context store ~1.6% of full-KV for those layers (exact arithmetic, model-dependent).

Each rank stores ~1/N of weights (doc: '1/8 of the model weights when using 8-way tensor parallelism'); KV also sharded across TP ranks for GQA heads. Adds NCCL buffer overhead per rank (rough: low hundreds of MB).

frameworks.hardware_support: NVIDIA GPUs only: Ampere (SM80+), Ada Lovelace, Hopper, Blackwell (sm100/103/120, incl. GB200/RTX 50). Quant format availability varies sharply by arch (see quant matrix); NVFP4 weights/KV are Blackwell-exclusive; FP8 GEMM compute needs Ada+. No AMD/Intel/CPU support.
frameworks.notes: Since v1.0 (Sept 2025) the PyTorch backend is the default architecture — models load directly from HF safetensors (weights memory = checkpoint size), no engine build needed; the classic TensorRT-engine flow (trtllm-build) still exists and bakes weights + fixed activation workspace into the engine at build time (engine file size ≈ weights; build phase peaks higher, rough ~2x weights for conversion). Overhead vs vLLM: comparable weight/KV math, but (a) the preallocation fraction semantics differ (free-after-load vs total-VRAM — see preallocates), (b) the TRT engine flow has a fixed, build-time-determined activation workspace instead of vLLM's profiled-at-runtime activation peak, (c) third-party benchmarks generally show TRT-LLM with equal-or-slightly-lower runtime footprint and 20-40% higher throughput after tuning, but these are workload-dependent and rough. KV pool example from official docs: 63.44 GiB pool = 259,840 tokens on an 80 GB H100 with a 7B-class FP8 engine. Key formula for the calculator: KV_bytes = 2 (K+V) × num_layers × num_kv_heads × head_dim × bytes_per_elem × num_tokens, rounded up to tokens_per_block=32 granularity per sequence.

corrections.claim: notes — KV pool example: '63.44 GiB pool = 259,840 tokens on an 80 GB H100 with a 7B-class FP8 engine'
corrections.was: Example attributed to an 80 GB H100 running a 7B-class FP8 engine
corrections.should_be: The official memory doc gives the log lines (1134.01 MiB execution context, 43.29 MB runtime buffers, 180.30 MB decoder, 63.44 GiB / 259,840 tokens — all verified verbatim) but does NOT identify the model, GPU, or precision. The H100/7B/FP8 framing is researcher inference (arithmetic-consistent at 256 KiB/token, but uncited). Also note that example log uses 64 tokens/block (259,840 tokens / 4,060 blocks), not the current default of 32 — label the example as 'doc example, model unspecified'.

corrections.claim: quant_support — 'INT4 AWQ (W4A16) — Ampere+; W4A8 AWQ on Ada/Hopper/Blackwell' and 'GPTQ ... W4A8 Ada/Hopper/Blackwell sm100/103'
corrections.was: AWQ W4A16 on all Ampere+ including Blackwell; W4A8 AWQ on 'Blackwell' generally
corrections.should_be: Blackwell sm120 (RTX 50-class / GeForce Blackwell) is NOT listed as supporting AWQ or GPTQ in any form in the official support matrix — sm120 lists only FP8 per-tensor, NVFP4, MXFP4, and FP8 KV cache. AWQ/GPTQ (W4A16 and W4A8) on Blackwell are sm100/103 only. Calculator-relevant: RTX 50 users cannot rely on INT4 AWQ/GPTQ in TRT-LLM per the matrix.

corrections.claim: quant_support — 'FP8 (per-tensor, block-scaling, rowwise) — Ada/Hopper/Blackwell'
corrections.was: Implies all three FP8 variants available across Ada/Hopper/Blackwell
corrections.should_be: Per the support matrix, Ada Lovelace and Blackwell sm120 support FP8 per-tensor only; FP8 block-scaling and rowwise variants are listed for Hopper and Blackwell sm100/103. Weight-memory math (1 B/param) is unaffected, but variant availability differs by arch.

corrections.claim: kv_strategy — tokens_per_block 'configurable 8/16/32/64/128'
corrections.was: Presented as the configurable set
corrections.should_be: Substantively fine but sources disagree on the range: the current kvcache feature doc says only 'a power of two greater than 1'; the legacy gpt-attention doc lists XQA support for 8/16/32/64/128; the early-reuse blog says blocks are choppable 'between 64 to 2 tokens' (the 7% TTFT result came from reducing 64 to 8). Default=32 verified in trtllm-build docs, llm_args.py (KvCacheConfig.tokens_per_block=32) and plugin.py. Recommend phrasing as 'power of two; commonly 8-128, default 32'.

corrections.claim: kv_strategy — '7% TTFT block-size tuning' attributed to the KV-reuse-optimizations blog
corrections.was: Attributed to 'NVIDIA's KV-reuse blog' (the introducing-new-kv-cache-reuse-optimizations post is cited adjacent)
corrections.should_be: The 7% figure is in the 5x-faster-TTFT early-reuse blog, not the reuse-optimizations blog: 'When running LLAMA70B on NVIDIA H100... speed up TTFT up to 7% in multi-user environments by reducing KV cache block size from 64 tokens to 8 tokens.' The reuse-optimizations blog contains only the ~20% priority-eviction hit-rate figure (verified: 'increasing cache hit rate by around 20%'). Both URLs are in the sources list so the substance survives, but the per-claim attribution is swapped.

missing: Weight streaming (trtllm-build --weight_streaming / gpu_weights_percent): offloads engine weights to CPU and streams at runtime, so GPU VRAM can be LESS than total weight size — directly changes the calculator's weights term for the TRT-engine flow. Verified in tensorrt_llm/commands/build.py ('Enable offloading weights to CPU and streaming loading at runtime').

KvCacheConfig.max_gpu_total_bytes: absolute byte cap on the KV pool (min of this and free_gpu_memory_fraction wins) — a third sizing knob alongside max_tokens and the fraction. Verified in tensorrt_llm/llmapi/llm_args.py.
KvCacheConfig.disk_cache_size: KV cache offload to disk (KV cache manager v2, PyTorch backend) — a second offload tier beyond host_cache_size. Verified in llm_args.py.

MLA models (DeepSeek-V3/R1): the stated KV formula KV_bytes = 2 x layers x num_kv_heads x head_dim x bytes x tokens is WRONG for MLA — TRT-LLM stores the compressed latent (kv_lora_rank + rope head_dim per layer, single copy, no separate K and V). A calculator needs a dedicated MLA branch or it will overestimate DeepSeek KV by ~10-50x.

Mamba/hybrid-SSM models (Nemotron-H, Mamba2 hybrids): constant-size SSM state per sequence instead of token-proportional KV for SSM layers; KvCacheConfig exposes mamba_ssm_cache_dtype (default 'auto') and mamba_state_cache_interval (default 256). Verified in llm_args.py.

Speculative decoding memory overhead: draft-model weights + draft KV cache live in the same GPU pool (e.g., EAGLE/draft-target) — adds a weights term the calculator should surface for spec-dec configs.
cross_kv_cache_fraction (encoder-decoder models): splits the KV pool between self- and cross-attention KV — niche but present in KvCacheConfig.
kv_cache_config 'use_uvm' option (unified virtual memory) exists in KvCacheConfig — minor, but relevant to a unified-memory-aware calculator.
verification.summary: Re-verified ~30 numeric/semantic claims against primary sources: official TRT-LLM docs (memory.html, kvcache.html, quantization.html, trtllm-build.html, useful-runtime-flags, tuning guide), GitHub source code (llm_args.py, builder.py, plugin.py, build.py, model_loader.py), GitHub release API, and four NVIDIA blogs. CONFIRMED exactly: tokens_per_block default 32; max_num_tokens=8192 / max_batch_size=2048; kv_cache_type defaults to PAGED with continuous/disabled options; KvCacheConfig.dtype default 'auto' follows checkpoint metadata (log message 'KV cache quantization set to \"auto\". Using checkpoint KV quantization.' found verbatim in model_loader.py, and the code path proves no unconditional FP8-KV auto-upgrade); free_gpu_memory_fraction default 0.9 = fraction of remaining FREE memory after weights/activations (memory.html: 'allocate 90% of the remaining free GPU memory'), 0.95 recommendation; enable_block_reuse=True, enable_partial_reuse=True, priorities 0-100 with default/offload-threshold 35, host_cache_size off by default; memory-doc log numbers (1134.01 MiB / 43.29 MB / 180.30 MB / 63.44 GiB / 259,840 tokens) all exact; '1/8 of model weights' TP quote exact; NVFP4 3.5x-vs-FP16 / 1.8x-vs-FP8 / 4.5 bits confirmed; NVFP4 KV cache 50%-vs-FP8 / 2x context / 3x TTFT / 20% hit rate / <1% accuracy / ModelOpt-required / Blackwell sm100-103 confirmed; 5x TTFT and ~20% priority-eviction figures confirmed; chunked prefill 'always enable, worst case minimal impact' confirmed in tuning guide; v1.0.0 published 2025-09-24 with 'BREAKING CHANGE Promote PyTorch to be the default LLM backend' confirmed via GitHub API; hardware = NVIDIA-only, Ampere SM80/86+, no Volta/Turing. Corrections are minor: the 63.44 GiB example's H100/7B/FP8 attribution is uncited inference (and that example used 64-token blocks); Blackwell sm120 lacks AWQ/GPTQ and non-per-tensor FP8 per the support matrix; one blog figure (7% block-size TTFT) is attributed to the wrong blog of the two cited. Biggest gaps for a VRAM calculator: no MLA branch in the KV formula (DeepSeek), weight streaming, max_gpu_total_bytes/disk_cache_size knobs, and SSM-state caches for hybrid models.

frameworks.name: MLX / mlx-lm (Apple)
frameworks.hardware_support: Apple Silicon only (M1/M2/M3/M4/M5 family) via Metal, using unified memory — there is no discrete VRAM; 'VRAM' = the GPU-addressable share of system RAM. macOS caps the GPU working set by default at roughly 2/3 of RAM on smaller-RAM Macs and ~75% on >=64 GB Macs (Metal recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize; e.g. 32 GB Mac -> ~21.3 GB GPU-usable, 64 GB -> ~48 GB). The cap can be raised with `sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=N` (resets on reboot; must stay below total RAM, leave headroom for the OS). MLX also runs on Linux/CUDA experimentally, but mlx-lm targets macOS/Metal.
frameworks.kv_strategy: Lazy step-wise growth, contiguous per-layer tensors — NOT paged. mlx_lm KVCache allocates in fixed steps of 256 tokens (class constant `step = 256` in mlx_lm/models/cache.py): on overflow it allocates ceil(needed/256)*256 new positions and `mx.concatenate`s onto the existing K/V tensors. So allocated KV ≈ tokens actually in cache rounded up to the next 256, with worst-case over-allocation of 255 tokens' worth (e.g. ~33 MB for a Llama-3-8B-shape model at fp16). There is no preallocation to max context and no paging/block table; growth via concatenate causes a brief transient peak of ~2x one layer's KV slab during each reallocation. Optional RotatingKVCache (enabled by `--max-kv-size N`) is a fixed-size sliding-window buffer constructed as RotatingKVCache(max_size=max_kv_size, keep=4) — it keeps the first 4 tokens (attention-sink style) and rotates the rest in place, hard-capping KV memory at max_kv_size tokens regardless of generation length (quality degrades for context beyond the window). No prefix/radix tree sharing across requests; instead mlx_lm.cache_prompt can precompute and save a prompt's KV cache to disk (safetensors) for reuse — saves recompute time, not VRAM.
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: Follows model compute dtype (fp16/bf16 = 16-bit per element) — KV quantization is OFF by default (`--kv-bits` default None). When enabled, KV is group-quantized with `--kv-bits` (8 or 4 typical; MLX affine quant supports 2/3/4/5/6/8) and `--kv-group-size` (default 64). Important default: `--quantized-kv-start` defaults to 5000, i.e. the cache stays fp16 until 5000 tokens and only then converts to quantized — set it to 0 to quantize from the start. Note quantized models still compute in fp16/bf16, so KV is 16-bit unless explicitly quantized.
kv_dtype_options: 8-bit affine group-quantized (--kv-bits 8, group 64): 8.5 bits/elem effective = 46.9% saving vs fp16
4-bit affine group-quantized (--kv-bits 4, group 64): 4.5 bits/elem effective = 71.9% saving vs fp16
other affine bit-widths (2/3/5/6) supported by mx.quantize; group size 32/64/128
--quantized-kv-start N (default 5000) controls when quantization kicks in
frameworks.preallocates: No memory-pool preallocation (nothing like vLLM gpu_memory_utilization). Weights load lazily from safetensors and KV grows on demand, so 'required VRAM' ≈ actual footprint: weights + KV(used tokens, rounded to 256) + activations + small runtime overhead. Two caveats: (1) MLX keeps a buffer cache — freed Metal buffers are retained for reuse, and the cache limit DEFAULTS TO THE MEMORY LIMIT (default memory limit = 1.5x Metal recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize), so process memory tends to plateau at peak usage rather than shrink (tunable via mx.set_cache_limit / mx.clear_cache). (2) mlx-lm's generation wraps work in a wired_limit context that calls mx.set_wired_limit(max_recommended_working_set_size) (macOS 15+), making MLX's memory wired/resident (not pageable) up to that cap, and prints a warning when model size exceeds ~90% of the recommended working set. Effective hard ceiling for the calculator: min(model fits) vs recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize ≈ 66-75% of RAM unless the user raises iogpu.wired_limit_mb.
quant_support: MLX native affine group quantization: bits 2/3/4/5/6/8, group size 32/64/128; per group stores one scale + one bias in the source dtype (16-bit), so effective bpw = bits + 32/group_size: 4-bit gs64 = 4.5 bpw (28.1% of fp16), 4-bit gs32 = 5.0 bpw, 6-bit gs64 = 6.5 bpw, 8-bit gs64 = 8.5 bpw (53.1% of fp16). mlx_lm.convert defaults to 4-bit / group 64 (mode_defaults['affine'] = (64, 4)) — this is the mlx-community convention behind '-4bit'/'-8bit' repos
FP formats: mxfp4 (gs32, e8m0 scale only -> 4.25 bpw), mxfp8 (gs32), nvfp4 (gs16, e4m3 scale)
Mixed-precision recipes via --quant-predicate: mixed_2_6, mixed_3_4, mixed_3_6, mixed_4_6 (higher bits for sensitive layers, e.g. embeddings/attention; mlx-community '3_6'-style repos)
Learned quants (same storage format, better quality): DWQ (distilled, best for 2-4 bit), dynamic (sensitivity-based 4/5-bit mix), AWQ, GPTQ — via mlx_lm.dwq / awq / gptq / dynamic_quant
GGUF: NOT first-class in mlx-lm; mlx-examples gguf_llm can read GGUF with native support only for Q4_0/Q4_1/Q8_0 (other GGUF quants are cast to fp16, inflating memory)

memory_overhead.description: Very low fixed overhead — no CUDA context, no preallocated pool, no compiled engine. Total footprint ≈ quantized weights (effective-bpw math above) + KV cache (lazy, step-256) + prefill activations + Metal/MLX runtime (~0.1-0.5 GB, rough). The dominant 'extra' is the prefill activation spike, bounded by chunked prefill (default --prefill-step-size 2048 tokens). MLX's lazy evaluation + buffer-cache reuse keeps decode-time activations small (tens of MB at batch 1). Remember the platform-level constraint: usable budget is recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize (≈2/3 RAM <64 GB, ≈75% at >=64 GB), not total RAM.
memory_overhead.activation_model: Prefill is chunked at prefill_step_size tokens (default 2048), so peak activation memory is roughly f(2048, hidden_size, dtype) and does NOT scale with full prompt length; rough rule: ~0.5-2 GB extra peak during prefill for 7-13B models at fp16 activations, scaling roughly linearly with hidden size and chunk size (rough numbers — MLX publishes no formula; lazy eval frees intermediates between chunks, mx.fast.scaled_dot_product_attention avoids materializing the full attention matrix). Decode-step activations are negligible (~tens of MB at batch 1). Add transient ~1 layer-KV-slab during each 256-step cache growth (concatenate copy).

savings_techniques.saving: Allocated KV ≈ used tokens rounded up to 256; max waste = 255 tokens of KV (~33 MB for Llama-3-8B-shape at fp16). Vs naive preallocation to max context: a 128K-context Llama-3-8B-shape model would need 16 GiB preallocated KV, MLX at a 4K working context holds ~0.5 GiB — ~97% saving in that scenario; in general saving = 1 - used/max_context.

8-bit KV: 8.5 effective bits/elem = 46.9% KV memory saving vs fp16; 4-bit KV: 4.5 bits/elem = 71.9% saving (group 64; per-group scale+bias in 16-bit add 0.5 bpw). Default quantized-kv-start=5000 means no saving below 5000 tokens unless set to 0. Disabled by default.

savings_techniques.technique: Rotating KV cache / sliding window (--max-kv-size N, keep=4 sink tokens)
savings_techniques.saving: Hard-caps KV at N tokens regardless of sequence length: KV_GB = N x 2 x layers x kv_heads x head_dim x bytes — e.g. --max-kv-size 512 on Llama-3-8B caps KV at ~64 MB vs ~16 GiB for a full 128K context (README: 'Smaller values like 512 will use very little RAM'); lossy beyond the window (quality tradeoff).

Bounds prefill activation peak to a constant w.r.t. prompt length (activations scale with the 2048-token chunk, not the full prompt); lowering it (e.g. 512) shrinks the prefill spike roughly proportionally at some speed cost. Rough: keeps prefill overhead in the ~0.5-2 GB range for 7-13B models even at 100K-token prompts.

4-bit gs64 = 4.5 bpw -> 71.9% weight memory saving vs fp16 (e.g. 8B model: ~16 GB -> ~4.5 GB); 8-bit gs64 = 8.5 bpw -> 46.9% saving; 6-bit = 6.5 bpw -> 59.4%; 3-bit = 3.5 bpw -> 78.1%; mixed_3_6/DWQ land between. Add ~2-5% for unquantized norms/embeddings depending on model (rough).

Saves prefill recompute time for reused long contexts (README: 'Caching prompts can substantially speedup reusing the same long context'); memory-neutral — the cache still occupies its full KV size when loaded. Not a VRAM-saving technique; do not model as one.

savings_techniques.technique: Raising the macOS GPU cap (sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=N) + mx.set_wired_limit
savings_techniques.saving: Not a memory saving but a budget increase: unlocks RAM beyond the default Metal cap (~2/3 of RAM on <64 GB Macs, ~75% on >=64 GB) — e.g. a 64 GB Mac goes from ~48 GB to a user-set 56 GB GPU-usable (+8 GB, ~17%). mlx-lm automatically wires up to recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize during generation and warns when the model exceeds ~90% of it. For the calculator: usable_VRAM = min(iogpu.wired_limit_mb if set, else 0.66-0.75 x RAM) minus OS headroom.

MLX retains freed Metal buffers for reuse (cache limit defaults to the memory limit, which defaults to 1.5x recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize), so RSS plateaus at peak; setting cache limit to 0 disables retention (frees memory back, costs allocation speed). Affects observed memory, not minimum required.

frameworks.notes: Calculator modeling summary for MLX/mlx-lm: required_memory = weights(effective_bpw/8 x n_params, +~2-5% unquantized layers) + KV(min(context_used, max_kv_size if set) rounded up to 256 tokens, x 16 or 8.5/4.5 bits per elem if --kv-bits, with fp16 below quantized-kv-start=5000 by default) + prefill activation spike (rough 0.5-2 GB for 7-13B, scales with hidden_size x prefill_step_size=2048) + ~0.3 GB runtime (rough). Compare against budget = recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize (≈2/3 RAM <64 GB, ≈75% >=64 GB Macs; overridable via iogpu.wired_limit_mb), NOT total RAM. No preallocation, no paged attention, no continuous-batching memory pool (mlx_lm.server batches but KV per sequence is still contiguous lazy-grown). Numbers marked 'rough' (baseline_gb, activation range, RAM-fraction thresholds at the 36-64 GB boundary) are estimates; bpw and KV math are exact from the storage format. Effective bpw formula: bits + 32/group_size (affine; scale+bias each 16-bit per group). Verified against mlx-lm main branch and MLX docs as of 2026-06-12.

corrections.claim: hardware_support / memory_overhead / savings_techniques: macOS GPU working-set cap fractions — 'roughly 2/3 of RAM on smaller-RAM Macs and ~75% on >=64 GB Macs' (boundary placed at 64 GB)
corrections.was: 2/3 of RAM for Macs <64 GB; 75% for >=64 GB
corrections.should_be: Boundary is between 32 GB and 36 GB, not 64 GB: recommendedMaxWorkingSetSize is ~2/3 of RAM for Macs with <=32 GB (e.g. 32 GB -> 21845 MiB ~= 21.3 GiB, confirmed) and ~75% for Macs with >=36 GB (e.g. M3 Pro 36 GB -> 27648 MiB = 75%; 48 GB -> ~36 GiB; 64 GB -> 49152 MiB = 48 GiB). The researcher's rule misclassifies 36 GB and 48 GB Macs (a 48 GB Mac gets ~36 GB GPU-usable, not ~32 GB). Calculator rule: frac = 0.75 if RAM >= 36 GB else 2/3. Note the researcher's own cited source (peddals blog) states the 64 GB boundary, but real device telemetry (llama.cpp ggml-metal logs, Apple dev forums) contradicts it for 36/48 GB machines. The researcher did flag this boundary as 'rough' in notes.
corrections.confidence: high for the 36GB=75% and 32GB=2/3 data points; the exact 33-35GB region is untested but no such SKUs exist

corrections.claim: default_kv_dtype / memory_overhead / savings_techniques: '--prefill-step-size' described as a CLI flag with default 2048
corrections.was: default --prefill-step-size 2048 (presented as a CLI flag)
corrections.should_be: There is no --prefill-step-size CLI flag in current mlx-lm (generate.py argparse has no such option; only --prefill-response exists). prefill_step_size is a Python API parameter of stream_generate/generate with default 2048 (verified at lines 316/1025/1213 of generate.py); the speculative-decoding path uses default 512. The memory-modeling consequence (prefill chunked at 2048 tokens by default) is correct; only the claim that users can tune it via a CLI flag is stale.
corrections.source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ml-explore/mlx-lm/main/mlx_lm/generate.py (grep: no '--prefill-step-size' argparse entry; 'prefill_step_size: int = 2048' in function signatures, '= 512' in speculative path)

missing: Speculative decoding (--draft-model, --num-draft-tokens in mlx_lm.generate CLI and mlx_lm.server): memory-relevant — adds the full draft model weights PLUS a second KV cache (draft model's own layers) to the footprint; a calculator should add draft_weights + draft_KV when enabled. Verified present in generate.py argparse and server.py (draft_model loading).

Batch generation / server concurrency: mlx-lm has BatchKVCache and BatchRotatingKVCache (cache.py) and a batch_generate API plus mlx_lm.server handling concurrent requests — KV memory scales linearly with batch size; the research only models batch=1 and mentions the server in passing.

Distributed multi-machine inference via mx.distributed (mlx_lm supports distributed generation/serving, e.g. sharding DeepSeek-class models across 2+ Macs over Thunderbolt/Ethernet) — changes the per-machine memory requirement; mentioned in mlx-lm README but absent from the research.
QuantizedKVCache programmatic defaults: group_size=64, bits=8 (cache.py) — consistent with CLI defaults but worth recording since server/API users may hit the bits=8 default rather than None.
LoRA/adapter loading (--adapter-path) adds adapter weights (typically tens to hundreds of MB) on top of base weights — small but nonzero for tight-fit verdicts.

MLX CUDA status nuance: as of mid-2026 mlx-lm does run text generation and fine-tuning on Linux/CUDA (pip install mlx[cuda13]); 'experimental, targets macOS/Metal' is acceptable but slightly understated — on CUDA the unified-memory/wired-limit budget model in this research does not apply (wired_limit is a no-op when Metal is unavailable, verified in generate.py).
verification.summary: Re-verified ~20 numeric/structural claims against primary sources (mlx-lm main-branch source files fetched directly, MLX official docs, mlx-examples). CONFIRMED exactly: KVCache step=256 with mx.concatenate growth (cache.py); RotatingKVCache(max_size=max_kv_size, keep=4) from make_prompt_cache (class default keep=0, but keep=4 is what mlx-lm passes — researcher's claim correct); --kv-bits default None, --kv-group-size 64, DEFAULT_QUANTIZED_KV_START=5000 (generate.py); quantize_model mode_defaults: affine=(64,4), mxfp4=(32,4), nvfp4=(16,4), mxfp8=(32,8) (utils.py); affine bits {2,3,4,5,6,8} and group_size {32,64,128} with per-group scale+bias in source dtype, so effective bpw = bits + 32/group_size (MLX quantize docs) — all derived bpw/savings percentages (4.5 bpw/71.9%, 8.5/46.9%, 6.5/59.4%, 3.5/78.1%, mxfp4 4.25) recomputed and correct; wired_limit context manager uses mx.device_info()['max_recommended_working_set_size'] and warns at model_bytes > 0.9*max_rec_size (generate.py, defined there not utils.py); memory limit defaults to 1.5x recommended working set and cache limit defaults to the memory limit (MLX docs); GGUF native support limited to Q4_0/Q4_1/Q8_0 with unsupported quants cast to float16 (mlx-examples gguf_llm README, exact quote match); QUANT_RECIPES = [mixed_2_6, mixed_3_4, mixed_3_6, mixed_4_6] (convert.py); DWQ/AWQ/GPTQ/dynamic with DWQ-best-at-2-4-bit and dynamic 4/5-bit mix (LEARNED_QUANTS.md); README quotes for --max-kv-size 512 and prompt caching; iogpu.wired_limit_mb behavior (resets on reboot, must be < RAM). KV arithmetic for Llama-3-8B shape independently recomputed: 128 KiB/token, ~33 MB per 255-token step, 16 GiB at 128K, 64 MiB at --max-kv-size 512 — all correct. TWO CORRECTIONS: (1) the macOS GPU budget boundary is ~32->36 GB, not 64 GB — 36 GB and 48 GB Macs get 75% (36 GB -> 27648 MiB), so the calculator's budget rule should be 2/3 for <=32 GB and 75% for >=36 GB; (2) --prefill-step-size is not a CLI flag in current mlx-lm (Python API default 2048 is correct; speculative path uses 512). MISSED items: speculative decoding memory (draft model weights + draft KV), batch-size KV scaling (BatchKVCache), distributed multi-Mac sharding, --adapter-path overhead. Overall the research is high quality and safe to use after applying the RAM-boundary fix.

frameworks.name: tt-vllm (Tenstorrent vLLM fork + vllm-tt-plugin on tt-metal)
frameworks.kv_strategy: Paged attention implemented with TT-NN ops (paged_fill_cache for prefill, paged_update_cache for decode, paged_scaled_dot_product_attention_decode). KV cache is a paged tensor of shape (max_num_blocks, num_kv_heads, block_size, head_size) allocated in device DRAM. Default block_size = 64 tokens (every LLM entry in release_model_spec.json uses block_size '64'; DeepSeek-R1 uses 32). CRITICAL DIFFERENCE FROM GPU vLLM: there is NO memory-profiling run. determine_available_memory() does not profile; instead num_gpu_blocks is hardcoded via cache_config.num_gpu_blocks_override. num_tt_blocks = ceil(max_tokens_all_users / block_size) + one extra block_size per user (worst-case heuristic: batch 32 x block 64 = +2048 tokens). max_tokens_all_users defaults to FALLBACK_MAX_TOKENS_ALL_USERS = 131072, with model/device-specific overrides (e.g. Llama-3.1-8B on N150 -> 32768; Qwen3-8B on N150 -> 32768; Mistral-7B on N150 -> 65536; Llama-3.2-90B-Vision on WH T3K -> 65536; DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B/Qwen2.5-14B on N300 -> 65536). Decode inputs are padded to max_batch_size and max_num_blocks for TT-NN trace capture (constant input shapes).
frameworks.default_kv_dtype: bfloat8_b (block-float 8-bit, ~1.0625 bytes/element) by default. In tt-transformers allocate_vllm_kv_cache, kv_cache_dtype falls back to ttnn.bfloat8_b when no optimization profile is set ('No dtype specified for the model KV cache - defaulting to ttnn.bfloat8_b'). The KV_CACHE TensorGroup is BFP8 in the default/performance optimization profiles. On the vLLM side cache_config.cache_dtype defaults to 'auto' (follows model dtype) but the on-device tt-transformers allocation governs actual stored precision.
kv_dtype_options: bfloat16 / BF16 (~2 B/elem, used in accuracy profile for precision-sensitive small models e.g. Llama-3/Mistral-7B/Phi3-mini attention)
bfp4 / BFP4 selectable via DecodersPrecision but not typically used for KV cache

memory_overhead.description: Per-device fixed overheads are explicit config values, not a single number. trace_region_size (DRAM region for captured TT-NN execution traces) is set per model/device in override_tt_config and ranges widely: ~30 MB (3e7) typical T3K/P150X4 small, 50-117 MB for Llama-3.1-8B variants (N150 52MB, N300 70MB, T3K 117MB, GALAXY 50MB), 215 MB (2.15e8) for Llama-70B on Galaxy, 256 MB (268435456) on P150X8, 384 MB (402653184) on P300X2, up to ~185 MB (184915840) for Qwen3-32B Galaxy. l1_small_size and worker_l1_size are L1 SRAM scratch overrides (e.g. l1_small_size 4096 for Gemma3, worker_l1_size 1344544 ~1.3MB on Galaxy). Model weights live in device DRAM at block-float precision (bfp8_b ~1.0625 B/param, bfp4_b ~0.5625 B/param). Host-side min_ram_gb is specified per model (e.g. 20 GB for Llama-3.1-8B, 175 GB for Llama-70B, 2.5 GB for Llama-3.2-1B).
memory_overhead.activation_model: Decode-phase activations are kept in L1 SRAM (small: batch<=32, single token per user). Prefill-phase activations are placed in DRAM because sequences are large; for seq_len>=1024 the FF input is reshaped to [1, seq_len//1024, 1024, -1] and attention reshapes for seq_len>2048, so prefill workspace scales with sequence length but is processed in tiled chunks rather than one flat buffer. No chunked prefill at the scheduler level (each step is prefill-only OR decode-only, never mixed).

frameworks.preallocates: Does NOT use vLLM gpu_memory_utilization-style fractional VRAM grab. There is no profiling run on TT. Instead it deterministically allocates exactly enough KV blocks to hold max_tokens_all_users (a fixed per-model/per-device token budget) via num_gpu_blocks_override. So 'required VRAM' = weights (block-float) + KV cache sized for that fixed token budget + trace_region_size + L1 scratch, rather than a percentage of total DRAM. Weights are loaded once into DRAM at startup (40 min for 70B, 10 min for 8B).
quant_support: bfp8_b (block-float 8-bit weights, default for WQKV/WO/FF and KV)
bfp4_b (block-float 4-bit, used for FF1/FF3 MLPs in performance profile and for >70B models even in accuracy mode)
GPT-OSS native MXFP4 MoE weights (gpt-oss-20b/120b supported)
block-float-2 (bfp2_b) exists in tt-metal data formats but not a standard LLM weight profile
frameworks.hardware_support: Tenstorrent only: Wormhole (n150 12GB, n300 24GB, T3K/LoudBox/QuietBox = 8 WH chips, Galaxy = 32 WH chips) and Blackhole (p100a 28GB, p150a/b 32GB, p300 dual-chip 64GB, plus P150X4/X8 and P300X2 multi-card). MESH_DEVICE env var selects topology (N150/N300/T3K/TG or mesh shape like '(4,8)'). Tensor-parallel and pipeline-parallel must be 1 (TT backend does distribution internally via mesh device); LoRA and speculative decoding unsupported.

savings_techniques.technique: Paged attention (TT-NN paged KV cache, block_size 64)
savings_techniques.saving: Eliminates contiguous per-sequence over-allocation; upstream vLLM PagedAttention reduces KV waste to <4% vs 60-80% in naive contiguous allocation and enables 2-4x throughput. On TT the block table also enables hot-swapping new users into a deployed model. Note: TT does not profile free memory, so it sizes blocks to a fixed token budget rather than maximizing occupancy.

bfp8_b stores KV at ~1.0625 bytes/element (16 8-bit mantissas + one shared 8-bit exponent per 16-element block) vs 2 bytes for bf16 = ~1.88x smaller KV cache (rough). Optional BF16 KV for precision-sensitive models trades this back.

bfp8_b weights ~1.0625 B/param (~1.88x smaller than bf16); bfp4_b ~0.5625 B/param (16 4-bit mantissas + shared 8-bit exp per 16-elem block) = ~3.56x smaller than bf16 (rough). performance profile uses BFP4 for FF1/FF3 MLPs; >70B models use BFP4 MLPs + BFP8 attention even in accuracy mode.

Reuses shared prompt-prefix KV blocks across requests. Gated per-model via model_capabilities['supports_prefix_caching']: enabled for Llama/Qwen/Mistral generators, disabled for models with sliding-window attention and for models that don't opt in (e.g. some Gemma/GPT-OSS). Savings depend on prefix overlap (no TT-specific number published).

NOT SUPPORTED. platform.check_and_update_config force-disables enable_chunked_prefill ('Chunked prefill is not yet supported for TT backend'). Scheduler runs prefill-only or decode-only steps, never mixed. So no chunked-prefill memory smoothing benefit.
